Creates the CodeAgent reasoning model with max_steps=2 to prevent hallucination loops
Defines the rag_with_reasoner tool: similarity search top-3 chunks, format, feed to R1
Prompt lets R1 return a better query to the primary LLM for retry, making it true agentic RAG
Ollama context-limit gotcha: default 2048 tokens causes hallucinations; build a model file to raise it to 8192+
Live demo: Qwen calls the RAG tool, R1 reasons over context, final answer returned

sources.title: Deploying Billions of AI Agents is Easier than You Think
sources.thesis: Packaging an AI agent as a Docker container makes deploying and scaling it to the cloud trivial, which is why a future of billions of configurable, business-customized agents is realistic.
sources.summary:
Cole opens with Mark Zuckerberg's prediction that there will soon be more AI agents in the world than people, then argues this is plausible because deploying and scaling agents has become genuinely easy. The vision he paints: a core set of reusable agents that businesses can instantly deploy and customize to their own needs, multiplied across millions of businesses and departments. The bottleneck people fear is production deployment, and this video is a practical guide showing that fear is unfounded.

The concrete walkthrough takes the GitHub code-Q&A agent (built with Pydantic AI and exposed via a FastAPI endpoint earlier in the series) and containerizes it with Docker. Docker solves the environment problem: a deterministic set of steps that installs the right Python version and packages into an isolated environment that runs identically anywhere, on any architecture or platform. Cole stresses he generated the Dockerfile with an AI IDE (Windsurf) rather than writing it by hand, and that you should always build and test the container locally first so you can tell code errors apart from cloud-config errors before deploying.

He then deploys the container to Render's free tier (chosen for ease and good Docker docs, not sponsored), highlighting that environment variables are the key to scaling: the same image can be run as separate instances (e.g. one for the sales department, one for marketing) each configured differently via env vars. Finally he deploys the Vite+React front end (from Lovable/Bolt.diy) as a Render static site, pointing its API URL env var at the hosted agent. The whole backend-plus-frontend deployment takes only minutes, and he closes by teasing future videos on scaling, adding RAG, and monetizing the agent.

key_moments.note: Zuckerberg's prediction of billions of AI agents; the video's framing
The vision: reusable core agents deployed and customized per-business = billions of agents
The GitHub agent (Pydantic AI + FastAPI) working locally; deployment is the hard part
The production problem: matching Python version, packages, drivers on the target machine
Docker introduced as the deterministic, isolated, portable environment that solves it
Dockerfile generated with an AI IDE (Windsurf), not hand-written
Test the container locally first to separate code errors from cloud-config errors
Many deploy options (AWS, GCP, DigitalOcean); Render chosen for ease + free tier
Environment variables as the scaling lever: per-department/per-client instances
Agent endpoint live on Render in ~5 minutes, tested via Agent Zero
Horizontal vs vertical scaling and load balancing teased
Deploying the Vite+React front end as a Render static site, wired to the hosted agent

sources.title: n8n + Crawl4AI - Scrape ANY Website in Minutes with NO Code
sources.thesis: You can deploy Crawl4AI as a Dockerized API endpoint and drive it entirely from an n8n workflow to scrape any website and build a Supabase RAG knowledge base with no code.
sources.summary:
This video is the no-code counterpart to Cole's earlier Python-based Crawl4AI tutorials. Instead of importing Crawl4AI as a Python library, he shows how to deploy it as a Dockerized HTTP API endpoint (using DigitalOcean's App Platform, or locally alongside an existing n8n instance) and then call that endpoint from n8n's HTTP Request nodes. Because Crawl4AI runs a full headless browser, it is a CPU/RAM hog and is best hosted on its own instance; an optional bearer token protects the endpoint.

The end-to-end n8n workflow discovers every page of a site by fetching its sitemap.xml, converts the XML to JSON, splits the URL list into individual items, and loops over each one. For each page it POSTs to Crawl4AI's /crawl endpoint, which returns a task ID (scraping is async), then polls the /task/{id} endpoint every 5 seconds via a wait node and an if-statement loop until the status is 'completed' and the markdown/HTML/links come back. The returned markdown is chunked (5,000-char text splitter), embedded with OpenAI text-embedding-3-small, and inserted into a Supabase vector store, with the source page saved as metadata.

He caps the demo at scraping the Pydantic AI docs (148 vectors across ~30 pages) and wires up a minimal AI agent (GPT-4o-mini + Supabase chat memory + the Supabase vector store as a retrieval tool) that correctly answers questions by pulling the right documentation pages. Cole frames the whole thing as a reusable proof of concept and stresses ethical scraping: check robots.txt and terms of use before crawling any site.

key_moments.note: Recap: last video introduced Crawl4AI in Python; this one rebuilds the RAG scraper in n8n with no code
Ethical scraping warning: check robots.txt and terms of use before crawling
Using sitemap.xml to discover every page URL on a site
Why Docker is needed: n8n can't pip-install, so Crawl4AI must be an API endpoint
Deploying Crawl4AI as a dedicated API on DigitalOcean App Platform from the DockerHub image
Config details: port 11235, bearer-token env var for endpoint security
Sponsor read: TEN Agent open-source voice/multimodal agent framework
Building the n8n workflow: fetch sitemap, convert XML to JSON, split into items, loop
First /crawl request returns a task ID (async scrape), not the page content
Polling pattern: wait 5s, hit /task/{id}, loop until status completed
Chunk markdown, embed with text-embedding-3-small, insert into Supabase vector store
148 vectors inserted; scrape is genuinely async (some pages need multiple polls)
Minimal AI agent (GPT-4o-mini + Supabase vector tool) answers Pydantic AI questions from the KB

sources.title: DeepSeek R1 Just Revolutionized AI Forever
sources.thesis: DeepSeek R1 is an open-source reasoning LLM that rivals OpenAI's o1 at a fraction of the cost, ships in distilled sizes you can run locally, and outperforms Claude 3.5 Sonnet as a coding assistant in a one-shot app build.
sources.summary:
Cole introduces DeepSeek R1, a newly released MIT-licensed open-source reasoning model that 'thinks' to itself before giving a final answer. He walks through DeepSeek's blog benchmarks showing R1 performing on par with OpenAI's o1 across most tasks while being dramatically cheaper (~$0.55/M input, ~$2.19/M output), and highlights that DeepSeek distilled R1's reasoning into smaller Qwen- and Llama-based versions so almost anyone can run a capable model locally. He shows that even the 14B distilled Qwen version is on par with o1-mini.

He demonstrates running the distilled models locally two ways: via Ollama (copy a run command into a terminal) and via Hugging Face (choosing quantizations for LM Studio and llama.cpp). He explains the anatomy of reasoning-model output: everything inside the thinking tags is the model reasoning to itself and should be stripped when you only want to surface the final response in an AI agent.

The centerpiece is a head-to-head coding test inside bolt.diy, his open-source bolt.new fork. Using the full 671B R1 through the DeepSeek API, he one-shots a chat interface for an n8n agent (with Supabase auth and history) for under a penny (~9,000 tokens), and it looks and works better than a comparable lovable build and better than the same prompt run through Claude 3.5 Sonnet, which produced a nicer-than-expected but non-functional result. The takeaway: powerful open reasoning models free builders from depending solely on Anthropic/OpenAI APIs.

key_moments.note: Intro to reasoning LLMs that think before answering; DeepSeek releases R1
DeepSeek R1 blog: MIT-licensed, fully open source, thinking model
Benchmarks vs o1/o1-mini/DeepSeek V3 (with grain-of-salt caveat)
Distilled Qwen/Llama versions; 14B on par with o1-mini
Pricing: ~55c/M input, ~$2.19/M output; dozens of times cheaper than o1
Running R1 via Ollama; 671B Q4 is ~400GB and too big for consumer hardware
Anatomy of reasoning output: strip everything inside thinking tags
Installing distilled R1 via Hugging Face quantizations for LM Studio / llama.cpp
bolt.diy test setup: build an n8n chat interface with the 671B R1 via DeepSeek API
R1 one-shots the full app for ~9,000 tokens (fractions of a penny)
Head-to-head: same prompt on Claude 3.5 Sonnet looks decent but does not function

sources.title: INSANE New Strategy for Building AI Agent Apps
sources.thesis: The fastest, cheapest way to build a custom frontend for an AI agent is to chain three kinds of AI coding tools together: a single-LLM builder (lovable/bolt.new) to lay a strong foundation, an open-source local editor (bolt.DIY) to iterate for free, and an AI IDE (Windsurf/Cursor) for final directed changes.
sources.summary:
Cole walks through his production process for building custom frontends for AI agents, using a GitHub code-Q&A agent (a Pydantic AI agent already exposed as an API endpoint) as the running example. Rather than committing to a single AI coding tool, he combines three tool classes to get the best of each: lovable (or bolt.new) is best at generating a strong initial foundation because it optimizes for one LLM; bolt.DIY is open source, runs locally, and lets you iterate with free models forever so you never burn out credits; and an AI IDE like Windsurf or Cursor is best for directed, fine-grained changes at the end. The workflow is: build in lovable, push to GitHub, pull into bolt.DIY, tweak for free, download the zip, then open in an AI IDE and deploy.

The core reason the workflow is so smooth is that the agent is decoupled behind an API endpoint, so the same agent code from the previous video is reused unchanged while different frontends hook into it. Cole's prompting philosophy is to be general about the UI (these tools design good interfaces without much direction) but very specific about the functionality and API contract: the agent URL, the input schema (payload), the output schema, the Supabase URL and public anon key, the messages table schema, and the fact that realtime is enabled so the frontend can subscribe to new messages. He ends every build prompt with a catch-all requirements dump for stray asks (markdown support, a loading indicator, Supabase email/password auth).

Practical gotchas and tips: paste the Supabase project URL and public/anon key directly (they are not secret), but never the service role key; bolt's web container blocks requests to a localhost agent so live chat only works in lovable and the downloaded local build, not inside bolt; you can drag/screenshot images into bolt.DIY/Gemini to give visual context; and the single best tip for iterating is to request one change at a time so the LLM understands and implements it correctly. The result is a free, flexible, fast blend for building all his agent frontends.

key_moments.note: Introduces the three tool classes: lovable/bolt.new (single-LLM builders), bolt.DIY (open-source local), and AI IDEs like Windsurf/Cursor
Reusing the prior video's Pydantic AI agent turned into an API endpoint so any frontend can hook into it
Why start with lovable/bolt.new: optimizing for a single LLM gives the best initial foundation
Why move to bolt.DIY: run it yourself with free models to iterate endlessly without burning credits
Walkthrough of the lovable build prompt: general on UI, specific on API/input/output schema
Bake the Supabase URL and public key directly into the code; include the messages table schema
Enable realtime on the messages table so the frontend subscribes to new messages
Full frontend built in one shot with lovable, auth and conversation history working, talking to the local agent
Publish to GitHub, then clone/pull into bolt.DIY (install via node.js, pnpm install, pnpm run dev)
bolt's web container blocks requests to a localhost agent, so live chat only works in lovable / local build
Key iteration tip: request one change at a time; drag/screenshot images for visual context
Download the zip from bolt.DIY, run npm install / npm run dev locally, then take into an AI IDE

sources.title: The Future of RAG is Agentic - Learn this Strategy NOW
sources.thesis: Turning standard one-shot RAG into agentic RAG - where retrieval becomes a set of tools an agent can reason about and choose between - solves RAG's most common failures and delivers far more accurate, consistent results.
sources.summary:
Cole Medin walks through why traditional RAG so often disappoints in practice - the wrong text gets retrieved, the LLM ignores context, and there's no chance for the agent to recover because a single vector search is a one-shot: the agent works only with whatever it was handed and can't decide it needs to search again or search differently. His fix is agentic RAG: instead of injecting retrieved context once, you expose retrieval AS tools the agent can call, letting it intelligently choose where and how to fetch knowledge.

The video is a full build. Using Crawl4AI he scrapes the entire Pydantic AI documentation (42 pages) from the sitemap, chunks each page while respecting code blocks and paragraphs, uses GPT-4o-mini to generate a title and summary for every chunk, embeds chunks with OpenAI's text-embedding-3-small, and stores everything (URL, chunk number, title, summary, content, metadata, embedding) in a Supabase table (245 chunks). Metadata like a 'source' field lets one table serve many agents via filtering. He then builds a Pydantic AI agent in three parts - dependencies, agent, tools - starting with a basic-RAG retrieval tool.

He demonstrates the limits of basic RAG: a simple 'what models are supported' question works, but 'give me the weather agent example' fails because a small chunk can't capture a whole example. Agentic RAG fixes it by adding two more tools - list all documentation URLs, and fetch the full contents of a specific page - so the agent can start with basic RAG, notice it was insufficient, list pages, reason about which one to visit (using titles/summaries), and pull the complete page. He also compares Supabase (RAG + structured data in one place) versus Qdrant (faster, vector-only), covers a Streamlit chat UI, and stresses that agentic RAG is extensible with more specialized knowledge stores and tools.

key_moments.note: Framing: RAG is the go-to for feeding external knowledge to LLMs, but it falls apart in practice (wrong text retrieved, LLM ignores context)
Thesis: of all the strategies (reranking, query expansion, rank normalization), agentic RAG works best
Weaviate article diagrams: basic RAG (chunk -> embed -> store -> match) explained
The core problem: basic RAG is a one-shot; the agent can't reason about or improve on what it got
Definition of agentic RAG: make RAG into tools the agent interacts with and reasons over
Chunking strategy: split pages while respecting code blocks, paragraphs, and sentences
Per-chunk title + summary generation with an LLM to help the agent reason about relevance
Metadata (source field) lets one knowledge table serve many agents via filtering
Live test - basic RAG works for 'what models are supported'
Live test - basic RAG fails on 'give me the weather agent example'
Adding agentic tools: list documentation pages + fetch full page content
Qdrant vs Supabase for RAG - vector-only speed vs unified RAG + structured data
Re-test with agentic RAG: agent tries basic RAG, lists pages, fetches the right one, returns the full example

sources.title: Turn ANY Website into LLM Knowledge in SECONDS
sources.thesis: Crawl4AI is an open-source, fast, memory-efficient web crawler that converts any website into clean markdown ready to ingest into an LLM knowledge base for RAG.
sources.summary:
Cole opens with the core limitation of LLMs: their knowledge is general and frozen at a training cutoff, so they fail on new tools like Pydantic AI. RAG (retrieval augmented generation) fixes this by feeding curated external knowledge into the model, but the curation/ingestion step is usually slow and painful, especially when the source is an entire website. The video positions Crawl4AI as the solution: an open-source crawling framework purpose-built to scrape sites and output LLM-friendly markdown, handling proxies, sessions, and irrelevant-content removal under the hood.

The walkthrough builds up in stages. First, a basic single-page scrape shows how ugly raw HTML becomes clean markdown via a tiny script (pip install plus a Playwright setup command). Then Cole tackles scaling: rather than hand-listing URLs, he pulls a site's sitemap.xml to enumerate every page, pausing to cover web-scraping ethics (checking robots.txt before crawling). He progresses from a naive per-URL loop (spins up a new browser each time) to reusing a single browser session for sequential crawling, and finally to parallel batch crawling (10 pages at once) that stays remarkably memory-efficient (peak ~119MB).

The payoff is a full RAG agent that is a Pydantic AI expert: Cole scraped all 42 Pydantic AI docs pages with Crawl4AI, embedded them into a Supabase pgvector database, wrapped a Pydantic AI agent around it with a Streamlit front end, and demos it answering questions (supported models, the weather agent example) with source links. He frames scraping-to-knowledge-base as the most common way to make an agent an expert, teasing a follow-up build video.

key_moments.note: The problem: LLM knowledge is general and frozen at training cutoff (Claude has no clue what Pydantic AI is)
RAG defined: retrieval augmented generation gives curated external knowledge to an LLM to make it an expert
Introduces Crawl4AI as an open-source crawler that formats output as LLM-friendly markdown
Core value: converts messy raw HTML into clean markdown; removes irrelevant content, handles proxies/sessions
Getting started: pip install plus setup command that installs Playwright under the hood
Scaling via sitemap.xml to extract every page URL automatically instead of hand-listing
Web-scraping ethics: check robots.txt before crawling (YouTube, GitHub examples)
Efficiency progression: naive loop vs reusing one browser session for sequential crawling
Parallel batch crawling: 10 pages at once, peak RAM only ~119MB
The gift: a full Pydantic AI RAG expert agent (pgvector + Supabase + Streamlit) demoed live

sources.title: The GLOW-UP Every AI Agent Needs
sources.thesis: A terminal-bound Pydantic AI agent becomes production-usable by wrapping it in a FastAPI endpoint with Supabase-backed conversation history and instantly hooking it into a free frontend (Agent Zero / the Live Agent Studio).
sources.summary:
This is the fourth video in Cole's series building a GitHub code-Q&A agent. Having already planned the agent, prototyped it in n8n, and rewritten it in Python with Pydantic AI, this installment gives the agent its 'glow up': turning the CLI-only agent into an API endpoint and hooking it into a polished frontend with conversation and chat history. The demo uses Agent Zero, a free, fully-local tool inside Cole's Live Agent Studio platform that lets you run an agent locally and instantly connect it to a hosted frontend with no third-party hosting.

The core engineering pattern is decoupling the agent from any single frontend by exposing it as an HTTP endpoint. Cole follows the Live Agent Studio developer guide, which supplies a sample Python agent template and the exact SQL to create a Supabase messages table for conversation history. The endpoint is built with FastAPI (served via uvicorn on port 8001), defines a fixed request/response schema (query, user_id, request_id, session_id in; a single success boolean out), verifies an HTTP bearer token for protection, adds CORS policies so the studio can reach the locally-hosted agent, fetches the last 10 messages for context, massages them into Pydantic AI's model-request/model-response format, calls the agent, and stores both the user message and AI response directly in the database.

Because the AI response is persisted to Supabase (with real-time enabled) rather than returned in the API body, the frontend gets instant updates and permanent, switchable conversation history for free. Cole frames the Live Agent Studio not as lock-in but as a best-practice starting point: the same API works for any custom frontend (the next video builds one with bolt.diy), and the whole thing can be submitted to a $6,000 hackathon.

sources.title: Pydantic AI + DeepSeek V3 - The BEST AI Agent Combo
sources.thesis: You can turn a no-code n8n agent prototype into a production-grade, model-agnostic Python agent using Pydantic AI and DeepSeek V3 through OpenRouter, keeping it powerful, flexible, and cheap.
sources.summary:
This is the third video in Cole's AI agent build series: after planning a GitHub code Q&A agent and prototyping it in n8n, he ports that prototype into a custom-coded Python agent using Pydantic AI. The core argument is that starting with a no-code tool (n8n, Voiceflow, Flowise) gives you a visual blueprint that makes the subsequent coding phase far easier, since the workflow's system prompt, memory model, and tool boundaries are already worked out. You don't always have to graduate from no-code to code, but Cole does it when he needs the control and customization that owning the code provides.

The Pydantic AI build breaks into three parts: dependencies (the HTTP client and API keys tools need), the agent instance (system prompt, model, built-in retry logic), and tools (plain Python functions decorated with @agent.tool, where the docstring tells the LLM when and how to use them). DeepSeek V3 is wired in by pointing Pydantic AI's OpenAIModel at OpenRouter via an overridden base URL and API key, which also makes swapping to Gemini, Claude, Qwen, Mistral, or GPT-4o a one-line environment-variable change. The agent gets three tools against the GitHub API: repo metadata, repo structure, and specific file contents (with a master-branch fallback the n8n version lacked).

Cole emphasizes a division of labor with AI coding assistants: they don't know agent frameworks like Pydantic AI well, so he follows the documentation closely for framework setup, but they excel at writing tool code against well-established APIs like GitHub, especially when handed the downloaded n8n workflow JSON as context. He closes by building a ~50-line CLI, showing careful conversation-history management (including tool calls so the LLM can reference earlier results), and demonstrating the finished agent answering questions about the bolt.diy repo.

key_moments.note: Frames the video: port an n8n prototype into a production Python agent with Pydantic AI + DeepSeek V3 via OpenRouter
Reviews the AI agent roadmap: plan -> prototype (n8n) -> database -> move to Pydantic AI code
Caveat: you don't always have to move from no-code to custom code; Cole does it for control and customization

Begins coding: imports, env vars, and setting the DeepSeek V3 default model

Pydantic AI has retry logic baked in for rate-limit / transient LLM errors
Pro tip: AI coding assistants don't know frameworks but nail tool code against well-known APIs like GitHub
Builds a ~50-line CLI to chat with the agent in the terminal
Conversation-history management: include tool calls so the LLM can reference earlier tool results
Demo: asks about a specific file; agent reuses cached repo structure instead of re-fetching
Swap the model by editing one env var (deepseek-chat -> gpt-4o); not tied to a model or framework

sources.title: How to DOMINATE with AI in 2025
sources.thesis: To stay ahead as AI accelerates into 2025, invest in a small set of high-leverage, tool-agnostic capabilities (agents, reasoning LLMs, local LLMs, tech-stack selection, working with LLMs) rather than mastering specific tools that get replaced every week.
sources.summary:
Cole opens by naming the core anxiety of the AI era: models, frameworks, and coding assistants ship every week, making it impossible to know where to invest learning time without it going stale. His answer is a single mental-model shift borrowed from a former VP: focus on capabilities, not tools. Capabilities are the higher-leverage, framework-agnostic skills (how to architect an agent, how to prompt a reasoning model, when to go local) that stay relevant as the underlying tools churn. You still must pick some tools to actually build, but mastering them should never be the priority.

He then walks a roadmap of the high-leverage skills he sees mattering most in 2025. AI agents are the headline: 2024's advances laid the groundwork, and the skill to build is agent architecture and best practices (citing Anthropic's 'Building Effective Agents' article and its patterns: prompt chaining, routing, parallelization, orchestrator-workers, and evaluator loops). Reasoning LLMs (o1/o3, QwQ, Gemini 2.0 Flash Thinking) are second: chain-of-thought / inference-time compute makes existing models far more capable and tackles hallucinations, and the future is agentic workflows that mix slow-but-powerful reasoning models with small fast models. Local LLMs are a discipline of their own (hardware, fine-tuning, privacy, cost, speed, flexibility), with the gap to closed models closing fast via DeepSeek V3, Qwen, and Llama.

The back half covers choosing an AI tech stack (keep it simple and DRY, don't over-research every option; he shares his own volatile stack as an example, not a prescription) and working with LLMs directly: prompt engineering techniques, using AI coding assistants, human-in-the-loop for agent approvals, and leveraging massive context windows. He ties it together with his vision of an open-source AI enablement stack and closes on a non-skill that he says is just as important: finding an AI community to learn alongside.

key_moments.note: Introduces the core motto: capabilities not tools
Story time: wasted a summer on advanced C++ (tool obsession) vs the next summer learning ML capabilities

Walks through Anthropic's 'Building Effective Agents' article and architecture patterns

Skill 3: local large language models as their own discipline
Skill 4: finding your AI tech stack; KISS and DRY
Cole shares his own full AI tech stack as an example

Closing: find an AI community; network is your net worth

sources.title: I'm Hosting a HUGE AI Hackathon Competition
sources.thesis: Cole announces a $5,000 AI-agent hackathon for the oTTomator Live Agent Studio, laying out sponsors, prizes, timeline, and the judging criteria for building a real-world AI agent.
sources.summary:
This is an announcement video launching a community hackathon competition centered on Cole's oTTomator platform and its Live Agent Studio. The challenge is simple: build an AI agent that solves a real-world problem, following a developer guide and using provided templates. Agents can be built as any Python agent inside a Docker container, or with no-/low-code platforms like n8n, Flowise, or Voiceflow, giving builders a wide range of supported stacks.

Cole frames AI agents as being in a 'wild west' era where the field is barely scratched, so contestants have room to build best-in-class work. Voiceflow sponsors a dedicated prize track for the best Voiceflow-built agents, and n8n is also involved. The prize pool tops out at $1,500 for first place with descending awards, plus Voiceflow-specific prizes, and a rule that each builder receives only their single highest-earned award.

The back half walks through the timeline (staged registration, build window, an initial judging round, then community voting culminating in a livestream winner announcement) and the judging rubric: innovation, functionality, usability, impact, and presentation. The Live Agent Studio itself is pitched as a community-driven, open-source platform where agents can be downloaded, studied, and reused.

sources.title: Revealing my COMPLETE AI Agent Blueprint
sources.thesis: Building a production-ready AI agent follows a repeatable end-to-end blueprint: plan, prototype in no/low-code, add a database, optionally move to Python, build a UI, test, deploy, monitor, and evaluate.
sources.summary:
Cole opens a miniseries by laying out his complete step-by-step process for taking an AI agent from idea to production, using an upcoming GitHub-repository Q&A agent as the running example. He stresses starting with deliberate planning (core functionality, which LLM, which APIs, and what a realistic V1 looks like) to avoid wasted effort, then rapidly building a working prototype in a no/low-code tool like n8n, Flowise, or Voiceflow before worrying about front end or database.

From there the blueprint layers on infrastructure: a simple Supabase/Postgres database for chat history and RAG, an optional migration to Python (Pydantic AI + LangGraph, aided by Cursor/Windsurf) when more customization is needed, and a clean UI built with Bolt/Lovable (React), Streamlit, or his Live Agent Studio. He then insists on real testing (unit and integration, to catch errors and edge cases) as distinct from later evaluation.

The production phase is containerizing with Docker, hosting on RunPod (GPU/local LLMs) or DigitalOcean (general instances), and fronting the agent with a FastAPI API. The final, interchangeable steps are monitoring (LangSmith, Langfuse, Logfire) and agent evaluation â€” verifying the agent gives correct answers and takes correct actions, not just that it runs. He closes by naming advanced enterprise concerns (prompt caching, token-window management, batching, load balancing, security, rate limiting, input sanitization) as ongoing improvements.

key_moments.note: Step 1 â€” plan your agent: core functionality, which LLM, which APIs, what a good V1 looks like
Step 2 â€” build a fast, powerful prototype in a no/low-code tool (n8n, Flowise, Voiceflow)
Step 3 â€” set up a database (Supabase/Postgres) for chat history and RAG
Step 4 (optional) â€” move the agent to Python for customization (Pydantic AI, LangGraph)
Step 5 â€” build a UI (Bolt/Lovable React, Streamlit, or Live Agent Studio)
Step 6 â€” test the agent (unit + integration) to cover edge cases
Step 7 â€” deploy: containerize with Docker, host on RunPod or DigitalOcean, front with FastAPI
Step 8 â€” set up monitoring (LangSmith, Langfuse, Logfire)
Agent evaluation â€” ensuring correct responses/actions, distinct from testing
Advanced enterprise topics: cost optimization, load balancing, security, rate limiting

sources.title: bolt.diy: The Future of Open Source AI Coding - What's Next?
sources.thesis: Fresh off making bolt.diy the official open-source bolt.new, Cole walks through the community roadmap, self-hosting resources, and a wave of new maturity features aimed at building the best open-source AI coding assistant.
sources.summary:
This is a community update and roadmap video for bolt.diy, the open-source AI web-app builder that just became the official open-source fork of StackBlitz's bolt.new. Cole frames the moment as capitalizing on partnership momentum and lays out a long-term vision: a per-LLM prompt library (because a prompt tuned for Claude 3.5 Sonnet underperforms on Qwen, Llama 3.3, or Gemini), file walking and diffs so the tool stops rewriting whole files, one-click deploys, better repo organization (kanban board, CI/CD, releases), and an extension marketplace/plugin system that lets contributors add features without bloating the core UI or editing the main repo.

He also previews more speculative roadmap items: a pre-planning step where a reasoning model like QwQ drafts a README/plan before any code is written, and an agentic 'mixture of experts' backend where multiple smaller LLMs collaborate to produce better results than a single call. The middle of the video points viewers to community-authored resources for getting started and self-hosting bolt.diy, recommending Cloudflare Pages as the deployment target (noting Ollama won't work for hosted instances since the site can't reach a local model).

The final third demos concrete shipped features from the maintainer team: a redesigned settings panel with toggleable providers and an experimental optimized system prompt, a debug tab that surfaces system info and connection status for troubleshooting, per-prompt token usage (prompt vs completion) so paid-API users can gauge cost, a resizable/full-screen preview, and the ability to screenshot part of the preview and feed it back to the LLM to fix visual mistakes. Cole closes by inviting the community to contribute ideas and pull requests.

sources.title: I Created the BEST AI Agent Platform in Just 2 MONTHS!
sources.thesis: Cole announces the pre-release of the Live Agent Studio, a community-driven, open-source platform where anyone can try, contribute, and eventually monetize AI agents built on any framework, unified by a standard payload and built-in chat history.
sources.summary:
Cole introduces the Live Agent Studio, a platform he built over two months as part of the oTTomator (automator) community. Its differentiator versus existing agent showcases is that it combines four things no other platform does together: every agent is open source, the community can contribute agents, agents are standardized so they can be built with any framework, and every agent ships with full chat and conversation history in a ChatGPT-like interface. Users get 100 free tokens to try agents directly in the browser, with a token system covering LLM/API infrastructure costs.

The platform launches with nine agents, some contributed by other creators (a business-idea researcher and a LinkedIn/X/blog content creator), establishing the community-driven model from day one. Cole stresses framework independence: agents can be built with n8n, Pydantic AI (Python), LangChain (JavaScript), or Flowise and still be compatible, as long as they follow the payload and conversation-history criteria in a comprehensive developer guide, which includes sample template agents on GitHub.

He demos a Pydantic AI web research agent (using Claude 3.5 Haiku) that searches and summarizes articles while streaming real-time progress updates, and an n8n content-creator agent that streams each asset as it is produced. The real-time streaming of intermediate steps is emphasized as what makes the agents feel interactive. Cole frames the studio as an extension of his YouTube channel where agents he builds in videos will be immediately playable, and positions oTTomator as a one-stop community for tools, networking, and knowledge in AI.

sources.title: BREAKING NEWS: oTToDev is Now the OFFICIAL Open Source Bolt.new
sources.thesis: oTToDev, the community-built fork of bolt.new, is becoming the official open-source version of bolt.new (renamed bolt.DIY) through a partnership between the automator community and StackBlitz.
sources.summary:
Cole announces that oTToDev, the community project he and the automator 'think tank' had been building as an open-source AI coding assistant, is entering a strategic partnership with StackBlitz to become the official open-source version of bolt.new, renamed bolt.DIY. He sits down with StackBlitz CEO Eric Simons to lay out the shared vision: bolt.new remains the stable commercial product for easily building anything, while bolt.DIY is the fast-moving, experiment-friendly, run-it-yourself version that supports swapping in any LLM (including local models) and pushes cutting-edge features first.

A recurring theme is that bolt.DIY has become a real-world benchmark for frontier model labs. Because it exercises models against actual full-stack application generation rather than synthetic evals, labs now ask 'can it run Bolt?' the way PC gamers once asked 'can it run Crysis?' Cole notes he personally test-drives every new model in bolt.DIY, citing spinning up Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B within hours of its release. A second pillar of the vision is education: teaching people how to actually work with AI coding assistants as co-pilots rather than assuming the model is broken when they get bad results.

Cole then covers the transition logistics: the automator think tank stays the community home, the repo moves into the StackBlitz Labs GitHub org (keeping the same stars, forks, history, PRs, and issues), the community retains full creative freedom over roadmap and direction, and commercial bolt.new will deliberately diverge from bolt.DIY so each can pursue its own vision while still sharing ideas. He acknowledges the sting of losing the oTToDev name but frames bolt.DIY (do-it-yourself) as fitting the run-it-yourself, local-LLM, cutting-edge goals of the project.

sources.title: We've Been Building AI Agents WRONG Until Now
sources.thesis: PydanticAI matters less as a specific framework than as a demonstration of the production-grade features (validation, dependency injection, retries, testing, logging) every agent framework must implement.
sources.summary:
Cole argues that the first wave of agent frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI, Swarm, no-code builders) aren't good enough for mature, production-ready agents without significant extra work, and that PydanticAI is starting to change that. Built by the team behind Pydantic (Python's validation library that OpenAI, Anthropic, LangChain, and LlamaIndex already rely on), PydanticAI puts validation at the core: LLM output validation, structured/streamed responses, model-agnostic providers, type-safe dependency injection for context (DB connections, API keys), retry logic, testing with mock/test models, and Logfire logging/monitoring.

The deeper point of the video is the 'how,' not the framework itself: any framework going forward must implement these often-overlooked features, and developers need to know how to leverage them to reach enterprise level. Cole walks the PydanticAI docs (weather agent example, provider swapping, dependency injection) then builds a ~100-line web search agent from scratch using the Brave Search API, defining dependencies (HTTP client + Brave key) so credentials never get passed through the LLM, binding a search tool via a decorator whose docstring tells the model when/how to use it, and setting model retries.

He runs it locally with Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B via Ollama (overriding the OpenAI base URL to localhost:11434), then shows a Streamlit version adding chat history and text streaming (noting Ollama didn't stream well, so GPT-4o was used). The closing message: staying current isn't about chasing the hottest monthly framework but understanding the direction of the industry and what genuinely makes agents production grade.

key_moments.note: Thesis: early frameworks (LangChain, CrewAI, Swarm, no-code) aren't production-ready without heavy extra work

Closing: stay current to understand where the industry is heading and what makes agents production grade

sources.title: Flowise + n8n - The BEST No Code + Local AI Agent Combo
sources.thesis: Pairing Flowise (fast no-code agent prototyping) with n8n (workflows exposed as agent tools), all inside a fully local AI stack, is the fastest way to build powerful AI agents that can actually do anything.
sources.summary:
Cole argues the future of AI is running everything locally â€” LLMs, RAG pipelines, and workflow automation â€” and demonstrates it by extending the n8n team's Local AI Starter Kit with Flowise, a free, open-source, LangChain-based low/no-code agent builder backed by Y Combinator. Flowise is his go-to for very fast agent prototyping because of its drag-and-drop node canvas, while n8n remains his favorite automation platform because of its integrations. The two pair perfectly: Flowise hosts the agent brain, and n8n workflows become the agent's tools.

He builds a tool-calling agent from scratch in Flowise â€” wiring buffer memory, a ChatOllama model (Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B, which he finds performs best as an agent), an in-memory cache, and a Brave Search web-search tool. A recurring theme is Docker container networking: to reach Ollama on the host you use host.docker.internal, and to reach another container (Ollama, n8n) you use its compose service name, never localhost. He also flags that every Ollama model defaults to a 2048-token context window, which silently truncates long histories and tool outputs, so you must raise it (e.g. 32,000) or the agent misses tool calls and hallucinates.

The core pattern is turning n8n workflows into Flowise tools: each n8n tool starts with a webhook trigger and ends with a respond-to-webhook node, and Flowise calls them via a Custom Tool node containing basic JavaScript fetch requests. Each custom tool needs a good name, an LLM-facing description (tells the model WHEN to use it), and an input schema (tells the model HOW to call it). Secrets like bearer tokens go in Flowise Variables ($vars.name), never hardcoded. He builds tools for Postgres table queries, Slack summarization, Google Doc creation, and Slack messaging, then chains them in one multi-tool request. Google Drive/Slack credentials require an HTTPS domain, so those tools point at his DigitalOcean-hosted n8n rather than the localhost starter kit.

key_moments.note: Thesis: the future of AI is running everything locally

Starts building a tool-calling agent from scratch on the Flowise canvas
ChatOllama config; host.docker.internal vs container name for base URL

Adds Brave Search web-search tool + credential; first agent test

Bearer token stored as a Flowise Variable ($vars), not hardcoded
Multi-tool chained request: summarize Slack -> web research -> Google Doc -> Slack message

sources.title: Our Bolt.new Fork's Most Requested Feature is FINALLY Here
sources.thesis: oTToDev, the community-built open-source fork of Bolt.new, ships its most-requested feature (importing existing local projects) alongside a wave of contributor-driven improvements that push it toward a mature, best-in-class open-source AI coding assistant.
sources.summary:
This is a community update video on oTToDev (the open-source Bolt.new fork, later bolt.diy) that Cole Medin is stewarding. The headline feature is the ability to import an existing local project folder into the browser-based platform so you can keep building on code you already wrote elsewhere; Cole demos uploading 13 files and shows the AI immediately understanding and extending them as if the code had been written in the same conversation. He flags a practical constraint of the WebContainer-based architecture: keep imports under ~100 files and exclude node_modules, because every file must be pulled into the front end before it can be filtered, and huge imports crash the site.

The rest of the video is a roadmap and contributor tour. Cole walks the high-priority roadmap items (loading local projects = done, GitHub repo import = in progress, attaching images to prompts = almost done) and credits specific community contributors for changes like reverting code to earlier versions, Cohere integration, dynamic per-model max-token limits, prompt caching, linting/testing/CI cleanup, and an auto-generated documentation page compiled from the repo's markdown. He gives a live demo of the in-progress image-to-prompt feature by checking out the PR branch via GitHub Desktop, reinstalling with pnpm, and uploading a screenshot for a vision-capable model (Claude, GPT-4o) to describe and attempt to replicate as HTML.

Cole closes by teasing a strategic partnership that will bring open-source expertise, financial incentives to reward contributors, and visibility/credibility to transition oTToDev from a hobbyist project into 'the real deal.' The throughline is open-source, contributor-driven development as the engine turning a month-and-a-half-old fork into a mature project.

key_moments.note: Announces the most-requested feature: loading existing local projects into oTToDev
Demo: importing a 13-file local project folder; AI understands all files
Constraint: keep imports under ~100 files, exclude node_modules (front-end must load all files before filtering)
Conversation controls: revert to a specific message and fork the chat
Roadmap: local projects done, GitHub import in progress, image attachments almost done
Contributor changelog: revert code, Cohere integration, dynamic max tokens, prompt caching
Live demo of in-progress image-to-prompt: checkout PR branch via GitHub Desktop, pnpm install, run
Vision-only: image upload works only with multimodal models (Claude, GPT-4o), not Qwen/Llama

sources.title: I'm Addicted to Windsurf, Send Help
sources.thesis: Windsurf's flow-state IDE fuses co-pilot and agent into one experience, and a handful of disciplined habits (resetting long conversations, one request at a time, scoping context with @-references, and switching models when stuck) turn it into a massive time-saver.
sources.summary:
Cole makes the case that Windsurf is the best AI IDE of the moment because it collapses the two separate processes of writing code and consulting an agent into a single 'flow state' experience where the agent is aware of every change you make and you can see every change it makes. He frames this against the history of AI coding: manual coding, then co-pilots (autocomplete) in 2022, then agents in 2024 (powerful but with unreasonable waiting periods, inconsistent results, and a disjointed IDE experience), and now flows that sync the human and agent timelines. He is candid that it does not produce production-ready code on its own, but insists that using it to lay groundwork, write tests, and document code frees up time to invest in the highest-quality work.

The bulk of the video is a set of power-user habits. When long conversations make Claude 3.5 Sonnet start hallucinating, start a brand-new conversation to clear context rather than fighting it. Give the agent one request at a time instead of spamming a list, because even a Chain-of-Thought agent confuses itself with too many simultaneous requirements. Take advantage of granular accept/reject of changes across files (changes are applied to the code immediately, so you can test before deciding). Use @-references to point the agent at exact files or functions so it edits the right thing. Be extremely specific, and define your tech stack up front (FastAPI, Python, Postgres/Supabase, schema) rather than issuing vague prompts.

He also recommends switching to OpenAI's o1 when Sonnet gets stuck on a hard bug (paste the code and error, get the fix, paste it back and continue in flow), using the function-scoped Refactor and Explain buttons to hone context on a single function, and leaning on the IDE to document code (telling it not to change code while documenting) and to scaffold test boilerplate. He closes by describing his broader workflow of building initial front ends in v0 or Bolt, then bringing them into Windsurf to finish, and predicting a near-future fully agentic IDE running locally with full machine access.

key_moments.note: Windsurf's core pitch: built to keep you in flow state, unifying agent and co-pilot
History of AI coding: manual (pre-2022) -> co-pilots (2022) -> agents (2024) -> flows (Nov 2024)
Cole's general workflow: build front ends in v0/Bolt, finish in Windsurf
Tip 1: long conversations make the model hallucinate; start a new conversation to reset context
Tip 2: give one request at a time instead of spamming a list of fixes
Granular accept/reject of changes; changes are applied to code immediately so you can test first
Use @-references to point the agent at specific files or functions

sources.title: Claude Computer Use + Bolt.new - The ULTIMATE AI Coding Combo?!
sources.thesis: AI coding assistants fail on new or niche libraries because they lack external knowledge, and Claude's computer use API can bridge that gap by autonomously researching documentation and injecting it into a tool like Bolt.new before the build.
sources.summary:
Cole demonstrates a core limitation of browser and IDE AI coding assistants (Bolt.new, Cursor, Windsurf, Autodev): they have no external knowledge of newer or smaller libraries, so they botch any app built around documentation the underlying LLM never saw. He proves this by having Bolt build a frontend around the relatively new N8N Demo widget library, which Claude 3.5 Sonnet inside Bolt fails completely, even failing to install dependencies and self-fix.

He frames the fix as 'RAG for an AI coding assistant': fetch the library's install and usage docs from the web and augment the coding prompt with that context. To achieve this today, he uses the Claude computer use API, which lets Claude control a computer (a sandboxed virtual machine spun up via the Anthropic reference Docker implementation) to type, click, and browse. With a bit of handholding (pointing Claude at the docs URL and supplying the workflow JSON), Claude researches the N8N Demo component, crafts a docs-augmented prompt, and drives Bolt to build the app hands-off.

The result works after one small correction (Claude initially included only one of the needed script tags): the app is deployed to Netlify (iframes are blocked inside Bolt's web container) and the N8N workflow preview widget renders correctly. Cole positions this as an experimental glimpse of a near future where AI researches to assist itself, either through computer use or via direct doc-fetching integrations built into coding assistants, aided by documentation authored specifically for AI to scrape.

key_moments.note: States the core flaw: AI coding assistants lack external knowledge like library documentation
Sets up a guaranteed-failure demo using the new/small N8N Demo library
Bolt completely fails to build the app, even failing dependency install and self-fix

Introduces the Claude computer use API as the mechanism
Shows Anthropic's reference implementation running via Docker in a sandboxed VM
Prompts Claude to research the N8N Demo component and inject findings into a Bolt prompt
Claude messes up research slightly (missing script tags); Cole hand-corrects
Deploys to Netlify to work around iframe blocking in the web container
Final app renders the N8N workflow preview widget successfully

sources.title: 10 n8n Tips in 10 Minutes to 10x Your AI Automations
sources.thesis: n8n is a powerful no-code AI automation platform, but the underutilized and trickier parts (production-grade memory/RAG stores, node referencing, looping, error handling, scheduling) are what separate toy workflows from scalable production ones.
sources.summary:
Cole walks through ten fast, practical tips for building AI automations in n8n, focusing on the parts people trip up on rather than basic workflow construction. His most emphasized point is production readiness: the default windowed buffer memory and in-memory vector store shown in most tutorials run inside the n8n instance and don't scale, so he recommends replacing both with Supabase (Postgres chat memory + the pgvector store) to stay on a single, scalable platform. He also gives a default LLM stack for quick starts: Claude 3.5 Sonnet for best quality, Groq/Llama for speed, GPT-4o mini for cheap-but-capable, and text-embedding-3-small for RAG embeddings.

The middle tips are n8n mechanics that matter at scale: different file types need different text-extraction nodes; referencing a previous node's output with json.* only works for the immediately preceding node, so you must reference a node by name to reach further back; and you can give an agent both a webhook node (as an API endpoint) and a chat trigger (for in-platform testing) by merging them with a small edit-fields expression that grabs whichever input is defined. He stresses careful handling of multi-item outputs, since n8n auto-loops over items one at a time and workflows must correctly handle cases like two files arriving simultaneously (e.g. concatenating both before sending to an LLM).

The final tips speed up development and harden workflows: data pinning preserves a node's test output across reloads so you don't have to re-run test events; a dedicated error-trigger workflow wired in via a workflow's settings can send Slack alerts when anything fails, giving production monitoring; the schedule trigger runs workflows on an interval for time-based jobs like overdue-task reports; and the n8n template library offers 1000+ searchable starter workflows.

key_moments.note: AI-agent recommendations begin: avoid non-scalable in-memory buffer/vector stores
Tip 2: default LLM stack recommendation by use case
Tip 3: extract text from different file types with different nodes
Tip 4: referencing previous node outputs (json.* vs node-name reference)
Tip 5: webhook + chat trigger dual entry point for an agent
Tip 6: handling multiple items in a single node output (looping)
Tip 7: data pinning to persist test output across reloads
Tip 8: error workflows for production monitoring via Slack
Tip 9: schedule trigger for interval-based workflows
Tip 10: n8n workflow template library (1000+ templates)

sources.title: oTToDev is Becoming the BEST Open Source AI Coding Assistant
sources.thesis: oTToDev, a community-driven open-source fork of bolt.new, is rapidly adding features (code streaming, an integrated terminal, provider auto-fetching, local-model support) and a clear roadmap to become the best open-source AI coding assistant.
sources.summary:
This is a project-update video for oTToDev (autod Dev), an open-source, browser-based AI coding assistant forked from the commercial bolt.new. Cole walks through recently merged community pull requests and demos the new UI features: automatic fetching of all available models from providers like OpenRouter (with pricing and a one-click 'get API key' link), out-of-the-box support for Ollama local models (fixing the default 2,000-token context limit), live code streaming so you watch the LLM type each file, and an integrated 'bolt terminal' attached to the workbench that surfaces the actual command output the model runs.

He then shares an initial public roadmap (built on roadmap.sh) and stresses that the project needs a clear vision so it doesn't die from implementing features 'willy-nilly.' High-priority roadmap items include: stopping Bolt from rewriting too many files, better prompting for smaller LLMs, loading local projects, attaching images to prompts, running backend agents (a mixture-of-agents / chain-of-thought system), one-click Netlify deploy, per-model prompt templates, and an extension marketplace so features can be toggled on/off without bloating the core UI. He also floats crowdfunding (a possible Kickstarter) to fund bounties on features.

The final section is a practical troubleshooting guide. The generic 'there was an error processing your request' message is unhelpful; instead look at the terminal (backend 500 errors) and the browser dev-tools console (frontend 400/404 errors). A blank preview is usually not an oTToDev bug but the LLM hallucinating bad code, file structure, or commands, so the fix is to use a larger model. The recurring lesson: smaller/local models mess up often, so use the largest model you can when building real apps.

sources.title: Turn ANY AI Agent into a Live Person - Voice Chat Setup in Minutes
sources.thesis: You can build and deploy a full voice AI agent with a Google Drive knowledge base in minutes, no coding, by chaining audio input, speech-to-text, an LLM with RAG, and text-to-speech in a no-code platform like Vector Shift.
sources.summary:
Cole walks through building a complete voice AI agent from scratch inside Vector Shift, a no-code AI automation platform he favors specifically for its strong voice-agent support. The pipeline is assembled as connected nodes: an audio input, an OpenAI Whisper speech-to-text node, a GPT-4o-mini LLM node, a knowledge-base (RAG) node querying documents ingested from Google Drive, and a text-to-speech node whose audio feeds back to an audio output. The key trick that unlocks voice is simply switching input/output node types from text to audio.

Along the way he demonstrates prompt templating (defining named `{{prompt}}` and `{{context}}` variables inside a node to accept multiple inputs) and how the LLM node can be swapped for essentially any agent â€” via Integrations, an API/webhook node calling a custom Python agent or an n8n workflow, or bridging to Zapier/Make for missing integrations. The finished pipeline is deployed and attached to a Voice Bot, which produces an embeddable chat widget (script tag or iframe) plus a standalone hosted page.

He live-tests the agent against his Google Drive knowledge base (it correctly retrieves silly action items from an '8/23 meeting notes' doc, though it botches the cited source URL), noting the response latency is slower than dedicated phone-calling platforms like Vapi. Finally he uses bolt.new (AutoDev) driving a locally-hosted Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B via Ollama to generate a vanilla HTML landing page wrapping the agent's iframe, working around bolt's web-container iframe blocking by pasting the generated code into JSFiddle.

key_moments.note: Why voice is hard: most platforms don't make voice agents easy; Vector Shift does

sources.title: Qwen Just Casually Started the Local AI Revolution
sources.thesis: Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B closes the gap between free, locally-run open-source LLMs and expensive closed models like Sonnet and o1, making capable local coding and agent workloads practical for the first time.
sources.summary:
Cole reacts to the release of Qwen 2.5 Coder 32B, an open-source coding model he runs entirely locally and for free via Ollama. He argues that until now local LLMs simply weren't powerful enough for real work, but this model changes that: he demonstrates it building a React + Tailwind chat interface inside oTToDev (his bolt.new fork) and driving a non-trivial LangChain/LangGraph agent, results so strong he had to check it wasn't secretly using o1.

The video doubles as a practical primer on running local models. Cole covers hardware requirements (recommending at least one NVIDIA 3090 for the 32B model), how to pull models through Ollama, and quantization as the lever to shrink a model's size (e.g. a Q2 build at 12GB instead of 20GB) at a small performance cost so weaker machines can still run it. He also points to the 14B variant as a lighter alternative.

To prove the model's edge, he contrasts it against Code Llama 34B, a slightly larger model that fails a basic build (never running npm install/Vite), and recalls Llama 3.1 70B failing the same agent tests. His custom agent exercises Asana task tools, Google Drive file search/download, and a local Chroma vector database for RAG over meeting notes, all of which Qwen handles well. The throughline: local, open-source AI is now good enough for always-on agents where paying per-token API costs would be impractical.

Primer on running local models: Ollama, the Qwen 2.5 Coder page, pulling the model

sources.title: The KING of No Code AI Agent Builders?!
sources.thesis: Voiceflow is a no-code AI agent platform that lets you build production-ready agents with fine-grained control by acting as the brain between a front end and back-end services, driving tools through intents and entities.
sources.summary:
Cole walks through building a production-ready AI agent with Voiceflow, a no-code platform he pitches as the sweet spot between full custom coding and simpler no-code builders: it gives you control over the RAG pipeline, conversation flow, tool integrations, and even custom JavaScript, while remaining code-free. He frames Voiceflow's mission as being 'the brain between your front end and your back end' â€” in his demo the front end is a React/HTML app and the back end is a single n8n workflow that posts a message to Slack, with Voiceflow orchestrating the conversation and deciding when to call the tool.

The core mechanics are intents and entities. An intent is the natural-language instruction (name, description, and example utterances) that tells the agent when to invoke a tool; an entity is a required input variable (like the Slack message text) that the agent must collect from the user before it ever calls the tool. Voiceflow's knowledge base is front-and-center for RAG (ingest a URL, sitemap, file, or plain text), and workflows are built in a drag-and-drop canvas with logic nodes, API nodes, reusable components, variables, and custom functions. Cole builds an intent 'send a Slack message,' defines the entity, wires a workflow that POSTs to his n8n webhook with a bearer token, handles success/failure branches, and tests it in a debug 'run' view that surfaces every intent, decision, and variable.

Finally he deploys the agent as an embeddable chat widget (or API endpoint) via the publish/integration tabs, and generates a simple front-end wrapper using autodev (his bolt.new fork) driven by DeepSeek Coder v2 236B through OpenRouter for under a penny. The takeaway: you can build any agent integrating with any service without writing the agent itself, while keeping the control needed for production.

key_moments.note: The problem: no-code platforms rarely give the control needed for production agents
Voiceflow's mission â€” the brain between your front end and back end

sources.title: How to Use Bolt.new for FREE with Local LLMs (And NO Rate Limits)
sources.thesis: You can run bolt.new-style AI app-building entirely on free local LLMs via the oTToDev fork and Ollama, provided you fix Ollama's tiny default context window and prompt incrementally from simple to complex.
sources.summary:
Cole introduces oTToDev (autod Dev), a community fork of bolt.new that unlocks using any large language model, including local ones through Ollama, instead of bolt.new's single hardcoded Claude 3.5 Sonnet. The appeal of local models is no cost and no rate limits, and Cole demonstrates that even a small model like Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B can build a full application well enough to serve as a strong starting point.

The video's core technical tip is fixing Ollama's default context length, which is only ~2048 tokens and too small to fit bolt.new's large system prompt, causing the model to chat in plain text instead of opening the web container and writing files. The fix is a small Ollama Modelfile (FROM <model> plus a num_ctx PARAMETER above 8192, recommended 32768) run through `ollama create` to produce a new higher-context variant that works dramatically better inside the tool.

Cole then shares prompting strategy: start with a very simple prompt to get a working (if ugly) baseline, then iterate with increasingly detailed requirements, including explicit UI/UX design details like colors, padding, and measurements, which guides even larger models better and minimizes hallucinations. He builds a Next.js chat interface that calls an n8n AI agent (webhook endpoint, RAG over a PGVector knowledge base) and notes model-selection tradeoffs: closed models like Claude and GPT-4o are strongest, but DeepSeek Coder V2 (236B) via OpenRouter or the DeepSeek API is 20x cheaper than Claude while still open source and capable.

key_moments.note: Intro: oTToDev fork of bolt.new adds ability to use any LLM including local ones via Ollama

Problem: local model just chats and never opens the web container or writes files
Root cause: Ollama's default context length (~2048) is too small for bolt.new's prompt

With the higher-context variant, the model opens the code window and builds correctly
Model selection: recommends DeepSeek Coder V2 236B as a cheap, open-source, capable alternative
Prompting strategy: start simple and iterate to more complex to reduce hallucinations
Second iteration: add detailed UI/UX requirements (colors, padding, measurements)
Third iteration: wire the app to an n8n agent webhook with RAG over PGVector
Working result: the local-LLM-built app talks to the n8n agent and answers from its knowledge base

sources.title: We're Taking this AI Coding Assistant to the MOON [Plus Installation Instructions!]
sources.thesis: Cole's rapidly-growing fork of bolt.new (being renamed 'autoDev') is becoming a community-driven, LLM-agnostic open-source AI coding assistant, and this video announces project updates while giving a step-by-step Docker installation guide.
sources.summary:
This is a project-update and onboarding video for Cole's fork of bolt.new, originally called 'bolt.new any LLM' and being renamed 'autoDev'. In just three weeks the fork exploded into a large community; the goal is to build the best open-source AI coding assistant, one that lets people use many different LLMs (cloud and local via Ollama). Cole covers announcements: a launch live stream, 36 applications to become core contributors, a Discourse community, and the rename.

The bulk of the video is a beginner-friendly installation guide. Prerequisites are Git and Node.js; you clone the GitHub repo, then configure API keys by editing the .env.example file (adding keys only for the providers you want, e.g. OpenAI or OpenRouter) and renaming it to .env. Cole strongly recommends running with Docker rather than natively, because Docker gives an isolated, reproducible environment that eliminates the 'works on my machine' class of errors (missing modules, access violations, header/API-key errors) that come from every system being slightly different.

The Docker workflow uses npm helper scripts that wrap Docker commands: 'npm run docker:build' to build the container image, then Docker Compose with profiles to spin it up for the development environment. The npm scripts are optional convenience wrappers â€” you can run the underlying Docker commands directly and skip installing Node entirely. Once running, you visit localhost:5173, and Cole notes a browser gotcha: bolt.new does not work in standard Google Chrome, so you must use Chrome Canary or Firefox.

Transition into the step-by-step Docker installation guide

sources.title: Ollama + OpenAI's Swarm - EASILY Run AI Agents Locally
sources.thesis: By overriding the OpenAI base URL to point at a local Ollama instance, you can run OpenAI's Swarm multi-agent framework entirely locally and for free using small tool-calling models like Qwen 2.5.
sources.summary:
Cole demonstrates how to take OpenAI's experimental Swarm multi-agent framework, which defaults to paid OpenAI models like GPT-4o, and make every agent run locally on your own machine with Ollama. The key insight is that Ollama supports function/tool calling in the exact same format as OpenAI, and exposes an OpenAI-compatible endpoint. So the only change needed is to instantiate an OpenAI client pointed at Ollama's local base URL (localhost:11434) with a dummy API key, then pass that client into the Swarm instance. No fork of Swarm is required.

He illustrates this with a SQL agent swarm from a prior video: a lightweight 3B router agent (Qwen 2.5 3B) that makes no tool calls except transferring, dispatching requests to specialized agents (an RSS-feed agent and an analytics agent) that generate SQL queries against a database using Qwen 2.5 Coder 7B. Splitting one overwhelmed agent into specialized agents makes each perform better, and each can carry its own instructions and behavior. A big advantage of Ollama over OpenAI is model choice: you can mix dozens or hundreds of models across agents rather than being limited to 4o-mini vs 4o.

The video closes with a from-scratch setup walkthrough: install Ollama from ollama.com (Mac/Linux/Windows), clone the repo, create and activate a Python virtual environment, pip install requirements, rename the .env example and set the model, then pull a tool-supporting model via 'ollama pull'. Cole stresses only using models with the 'tools' label since non-tool models cannot act as agents, and notes that small 3B/7B models run well even on a laptop 3060 GPU while larger 32B+ models need a 3080/3090. Local performance still trails GPT, but the gap is closing fast.

key_moments.note: Intro: OpenAI's Swarm makes multi-agent systems easy but defaults to paid OpenAI models; goal is to run swarms locally with Ollama for free
Ollama added tool support (July 25) with OpenAI compatibility â€” override the base URL to route requests to Ollama instead of OpenAI
Swarm's default client has no base URL override; the only change is to pass in a custom OpenAI client pointed at Ollama

Live test: how many users on the platform â†’ routes to user agent, runs a count, answers 'five users'
Harder analytics test: most popular RSS feed â†’ analytics agent generates a JOIN query, returns correct answer
From-scratch setup: install Ollama, clone repo, venv, pip install requirements, set .env
Pulling models: only use models with the 'tools' label from ollama.com/search; ollama pull <model>

sources.title: This Bolt.new Fork is EXPLODING - Don't Miss out on What's Happening!
sources.thesis: Cole's open-source fork of bolt.new is rapidly becoming a community-driven project whose core mission is to let any LLM (especially local ones) power an in-browser AI coding assistant, with backend agentic workflows as the most exciting next frontier.
sources.summary:
Two weeks after releasing a fork of bolt.new whose original purpose was to break the official version's single-model limitation and support any LLM (especially local ones), Cole reports the project has exploded into a genuine open-source community with 20 pull requests in a single week. He announces four updates: a long-term maintenance commitment, an upcoming Discourse community, applications to become a core contributor/maintainer, and regular (possibly weekly) content plus a setup tutorial built around Docker.

He then walks the README changelog of recently merged contributions: Chain-of-Thought / structured-planning prompt improvements, DeepSeek and Mistral API integrations, and a generic OpenAI-compatible API integration (swap the base URL so any OpenAI-compatible provider like Groq, Fireworks, or Together works through one env var). Additional merged features include one-way file sync to a local folder, Docker containerization, and publishing generated projects directly to GitHub, all credited to specific contributors.

Finally he lays out the roadmap and high-priority items: better prompting so smaller/local models reliably open the web container, native image attachments, and the standout ambition of running true agentic workflows in the backend (multi-model interaction, self-reflection, self-improvement) rather than a single model call, plus integrations (LM Studio, Azure OpenAI, Hugging Face, perplexity), deploy-to-Vercel/Netlify, code reverting, prompt caching, in-UI API keys, and having the LLM write its plan to a markdown file in the project.

sources.title: STOP Paying for GPT, Claude, & Other LLMs - Get them ALL in One Place
sources.thesis: Instead of paying for separate GPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama subscriptions, a single unified multi-model chat platform (ChatLLM by Abacus.AI) gives access to all the top LLMs plus extra features for a fraction of the cost.
sources.summary:
Cole frames a common pain point for AI developers: the best coding tools (Bolt.new, Fragment for scaffolding; Cursor, Cline for in-codebase edits) still leave a gap where you just need a ChatGPT-style interface to reason through a specific or high-level problem. But every frontier LLM lives on its own platform behind its own $20/month subscription, so power users end up paying for and juggling several. He pitches ChatLLM (chatllm.abacus.com) as a single $10/month platform that exposes all the top models (Claude 3.5 Sonnet, GPT-4o, Llama 3.1 405B, Gemini 1.5 Pro, o1) without the punishing rate limits.

The bulk of the video is a feature walkthrough demonstrating that you not only lose nothing by consolidating but gain capabilities the native apps lack: web search, document/image attachment (including from OneDrive/Google Drive), artifacts/playground code preview that works even under GPT-4o (which ChatGPT itself doesn't offer for GPT), audio and video summarization (transcribing both audio and visual content), image-to-code generation, image and video generation, PowerPoint and doc generation, URL scraping, and screenshotting your screen for context.

A recurring theme is that the platform's explicit action buttons (generate code, generate image, take screenshot, etc.) let you deliberately direct the LLM toward the output you want rather than hoping the default chat interface infers it. Cole closes by noting ChatLLM is team-based (invite collaborators, share prompts/conversations) and connects to external platforms like Teams, Jira, Confluence, Google Drive, and Slack via connectors.

key_moments.note: AI dev tool landscape: Bolt.new/Fragment scaffold codebases, Cursor/Cline edit in-codebase, but you still need a chat interface for specific/high-level problems
The pain: every LLM lives on a separate platform, each ~$20/month

sources.title: Use Open WebUI with Your N8N AI Agents - Voice Chat Included!
sources.thesis: You can extend the self-hosted local-AI starter kit by adding Open WebUI and a custom function so its ChatGPT-like interface (including voice) talks directly to your n8n agent workflows via a webhook.
sources.summary: Cole extends the n8n team's self-hosted AI starter kit â€” a Docker Compose bundle of n8n (workflow/agent builder), Ollama (local LLMs), Qdrant (vector DB for RAG), and Postgres (SQL + chat memory) â€” by adding a fifth container: Open WebUI. Open WebUI is an open-source ChatGPT-like interface normally used to chat with Ollama models, but its 'functions' and 'pipelines' extension points let you route chats to arbitrary agents and API endpoints. Cole wrote (and published to the Open WebUI function list) an 'n8n pipe' function that makes an n8n workflow appear as a selectable model alongside your Ollama models.

key_moments.note: Recap: prior video ran all AI locally via the self-hosted AI starter kit; the missing piece was Open WebUI
