sources.title: The MOST Profitable AI Niche NO ONE is Talking About
sources.thesis: Building AI agents on top of GoHighLevel â€” a feature-rich but AI-weak CRM for marketing agencies â€” is a lucrative, underserved niche, and lead-nurturing agents are the single biggest opportunity.
sources.summary:
Cole argues that the most profitable AI-agent niche is not a technology but a platform: GoHighLevel (GHL), an all-in-one CRM/marketing suite used by thousands of agencies. Because GHL is a 'jack of all trades, master of none,' every one of its features (landing pages, lead capture, email campaigns, scheduling, reviews) is good-but-imperfect, leaving gaps a developer can fill with AI. Its strong, developer-first API plus an app Marketplace (with built-in auth, webhooks, and permissions) actively encourage third-party builders, making it 'the perfect storm' for either a SaaS product or an agency-consultancy model.

The standout opportunity is personalized lead nurturing at scale â€” speed, volume, and personalization are the name of the game in digital marketing, and AI agents are ideal for holding thousands of tailored conversations to drive purchases, bookings, and appointments. Cole demonstrates a real agent he built (branded 'Textually') using LangChain, LangGraph, and Pinecone for RAG. A GHL Marketplace app registers a webhook (pointed at a Google Cloud Function) that fires on inbound/outbound SMS; the function authenticates, resolves the agency (company ID) and contact (location/sub-account), and uses GHL tags to route the message to the correct per-client agent.

Each agent has its own prompt, model, temperature, and custom actions defined in natural language through a front end. The LangGraph executable binds tools that act on GHL (add/remove tags, invoke webhooks, fetch calendar availability, book/cancel appointments). Prompts are built dynamically per message â€” injecting RAG'd FAQs, location context (address, phone), the natural-language actions, date/timezone info to prevent hallucinated dates, and the base goal prompt â€” so a single platform serves very different clients (solar, roofing, chiropractic) from separate sub-accounts.

key_moments.note: Reveals the niche: building AI agents for GoHighLevel, a CRM for marketing agencies
'Jack of all trades, master of none' framed as the opportunity to fill gaps with AI
GHL's strong API + app Marketplace encourage developers to extend the platform
Digital marketing = speed, volume, personalization; AI agents are perfect for lead nurturing
Cole's own agent: LangChain + LangGraph + Pinecone for RAG
Marketplace app webhook setup â€” inbound/outbound SMS fires a Google Cloud Function
Per-client agents live in GHL sub-accounts with different prompts/models/temperature
Tag-based routing determines which agent handles a given contact's message
LangGraph executable with bound tools: tags, webhooks, calendar, book/cancel appointments
Dynamic prompt construction â€” FAQs, location context, actions, date/timezone injected per message

sources.title: You're WASTING TIME Coding Your AI Agents
sources.thesis: You do not need to code every AI agent from scratch: n8n lets you build powerful, self-hosted, no-code AI agent workflows with LangChain integration so you can reserve hand-coding for only the most complex agents.
sources.summary:
Cole argues that while coding your own AI agents gives maximum control, it costs a lot of time and debugging, and for simpler agents a no-code workflow tool is a better use of effort. He makes the case for n8n specifically over competitors like Zapier and Make.com for two reasons: it can be self-hosted (turning per-execution costs into a flat ~$6/month cloud VM bill on DigitalOcean, versus the ~$174/month he once paid Zapier), and it has direct LangChain integration so any workflow can embed a custom chain with any LLM, chat memory, tools, and structured output.

He demonstrates by recreating a basic version of his Asana task-management agent (from his AI Agents Master Class series) inside n8n with no code. A workflow is a graph of nodes, each either a trigger or an action; one workflow creates an Asana task from a dynamic name and due date, and a second workflow wraps an AI agent whose entry point is a chat input. The agent node hooks up an OpenAI model, a window buffer memory, and tools -- one tool references the task-creation workflow by ID, with a description and structured input (name + due date) that tell the agent how to call it. A 'compute date/time' node feeds the current date into the system message so the agent resolves relative dates like 'this Friday.'

He shows the agent working end-to-end, then exposes it two ways: as an API endpoint (called from Streamlit) and as an embeddable chat widget that n8n generates as ready-to-paste HTML/React/Vue, with public access and optional authentication. Live, it creates a real 'bake cookies by Friday' Asana task. His closing posture: use no-code for simpler agents so you can dedicate real coding time to the cutting-edge, complex agents that truly require it.

key_moments.note: Pitches n8n as a no-code way to build powerful AI agent workflows
Reason 1 n8n wins: self-hosting means $0 per-execution cost at any scale
Reason 2: direct LangChain integration for custom chains, any LLM, memory, tools
Self-hosting walkthrough: DigitalOcean droplet at ~$6/month
Explains n8n workflows as graphs of trigger + action nodes
The Asana task-creation workflow with dynamic name and due date
Building the AI agent node: OpenAI model, window buffer memory, tools
Tool = reference to the task workflow with description + structured input
Feeding current date into the system message for relative-date resolution
Exposing the agent as an API endpoint and as an embeddable chat widget
Live demo: agent creates a real 'bake cookies by Friday' Asana task
Closing posture: no-code for simple agents, save coding for complex ones

sources.title: Build a Chatbot for Your Website in 4 Minutes (No Code)
sources.thesis: Building a custom RAG-powered website chatbot trained on your own data is a use case worth handling with a no-code product like Folder rather than coding it yourself.
sources.summary:
Cole argues that while he usually teaches coding AI agents from scratch, some use cases are better served by an off-the-shelf product, and a custom RAG website chatbot is one of them. Nearly every business with a website needs a chatbot that can answer questions about its products and services from its own data, and doing this with no code in under five minutes beats building it by hand.

He demonstrates Folder end-to-end: create an account, make an 'assistant' (choosing an AI model like Claude/GPT/Llama, writing a base prompt/system message, and picking a personality), then upload documents (PDFs, text, Google Drive, YouTube, a JSON API) to train it for RAG. Training the assistant on five documents takes about 20 seconds, after which it correctly answers a question that could only be answered from the uploaded meeting notes. He then wires the assistant into a website chatbot, customizes branding, lead capture, and popup placement, and grabs an embed snippet to paste into any site.

Finally he shows Folder's workflow builder, where an assistant can be chained into automations, for example pulling action items from a meeting and texting them via SMS. He notes the differentiator versus n8n, Zapier, and Make is that the automations can leverage the assistants built inside the same platform, with integrations for SMS, Google Drive, and Slack.

key_moments.note: Custom RAG website chatbots framed as one of the most common AI agent use cases
Argues some things are worth using a product for instead of coding yourself
Creating an assistant: name, AI model, base prompt (system message), personality
Uploading PDF/text files (plus Drive/YouTube/JSON API) to train RAG
Training finishes in ~20 seconds; answers a doc-only question correctly
Wiring the assistant into a website chatbot with branding, lead capture, embed code
Workflow view: assistant chained into an SMS automation for meeting action items

sources.title: THE BEST Tool for AI Agent Workflows - LangGraph FULL Guide
sources.thesis: LangGraph gives production-grade AI agent applications a clear, stateful graph structure (state, nodes, edges) that turns spaghetti multi-agent code into clean, scalable, extensible agentic workflows.
sources.summary:
Cole introduces LangGraph, LangChain's library for building stateful multi-actor agentic applications, as the antidote to the spaghetti code developers write when they try to wire multiple agents and tool loops together by hand. He frames LangGraph around three core concepts: the graph state (all variables tracked across execution, like chat history), the nodes (the agents themselves), and the edges (how nodes connect, including conditional edges/routers). Key features like cycles and branching, persistence, human-in-the-loop approval, and streaming support are what make complex workflows manageable.

He walks through three example workflows to build intuition: a chart-generation flow with a researcher and a router deciding whether to research more or generate the chart; a code-execution flow that generates imports and code, retries on failure, and returns the final answer; and the simplest agent-tool loop where an agent repeatedly decides whether to call tools or respond to the user. He then refactors the recursive prompt_ai function from his Asana task-management agent into a LangGraph runnable, showing how the annotated add_messages state, a call-model node (async for streaming), a tool node, and a conditional 'should continue' router come together into a compiled app.

Finally he wires the runnable into a Streamlit UI, using async stream_events to yield typewriter-style chunks, a thread ID plus a SQLite checkpointer for state persistence across executions, and a system message for agent context. He tests it live against Asana (listing projects, creating a task), confirms parity with the old implementation, and teases future content on human-in-the-loop and deploying LangGraph runnables to the cloud via LangServe.

key_moments.note: Opens on the spaghetti-code problem of tying agents together and pitches LangGraph as the fix
Tours the LangGraph docs; defines it as a library for stateful multi-actor LLM apps
Lists key features: cycles/branching, persistence, human-in-the-loop, streaming, LangChain integration
Explains the three core concepts: graph, state, nodes, edges, checkpoints, threads
Example 1: chart-generation workflow with researcher + router
Example 2: code-execution workflow with retry-on-failure loop
Example 3: the agent-tool loop that the task manager will implement
Shows the original recursive prompt_ai function and why it does not extend
Model selection: build ChatOpenAI or ChatAnthropic based on config, bind tools
Defines the graph state with the add_messages annotated list
Builds the call-model node (async for streaming) and the tool node
Defines the router / conditional edge (should_continue) returning END or 'tools'
Compiles nodes, entry point, and conditional edge into the app workflow
Adds SQLite-based persistence via a checkpointer at compile time
Wires the runnable into the Streamlit UI with stream_events and thread ID
Live test against Asana: list projects, create a task, confirm streaming + memory

sources.title: \"Prompt Engineering\" is a Bunch of BS - Here's the 5% of it to Learn
sources.thesis: Most prompt engineering is fluff; learn a handful of core techniques in a few hours, then use a simple four-part prompt template and go build.
sources.summary:
Cole argues that prompt engineering has been massively over-hyped, to the point of people chasing $250k-$300k 'prompt engineer' jobs, when in reality only about 5% of it is worth learning. He points to a single free resource, the Prompt Engineering Guide, and recommends studying it for just 3-5 hours rather than sinking dozens or hundreds of hours into mastering every technique and crafting 'perfect' prompts.

He names the specific techniques worth knowing: the first six basics (zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, self-consistency, general knowledge prompting, and prompt chaining), plus two that matter most for building AI agents: ReAct (reason + act), the framework agents use to reason about invoking tools and relaying responses, and reflection (self-reflection), where an AI improves its own output. These, he stresses, are the 'key' parts, not fluff, and knowing them covers 95-99% of what you'll want to do with AI without resorting to hacks or jailbreaks.

He closes with his own reusable template for more complex prompts: four simple categories, a system prompt (who the agent is, with descriptive adjectives), a goal, a list of things to do, and a list of things not to do. This structure has worked well for the many AI agents he has built, letting him slot in any niche instruction or hallucination-avoidance rule without over-engineering the prompt.

key_moments.note: Opens by mocking the obsession with prompt engineering and inflated 'prompt engineer' salaries
Recommends the Prompt Engineering Guide as the single resource; study only 3-5 hours
Names the six basic techniques worth learning (zero-shot, few-shot, CoT, self-consistency, general knowledge, prompt chaining)
Highlights ReAct (reason + act) as the framework agents use with tools, plus reflection
Shares his four-part prompt template: system prompt, goal, things to do, things not to do

sources.title: This AI Agent with RAG Manages MY LIFE
sources.thesis: An AI agent becomes genuinely useful for daily life when it can intelligently decide whether to search documents via RAG and then act on what it finds through external tools like Asana.
sources.summary:
Cole walks through upgrading a basic RAG chatbot into a practical AI agent that searches his meeting notes and creates Asana tasks from the action items it finds. He calls out three flaws in the earlier RAG-only implementation: it always ran RAG regardless of the prompt, it did nothing beyond RAG (no other tools/APIs), and it rebuilt the vector database in memory from scratch on every run. The fix is to make retrieval agentic (the LLM decides when to search), to combine RAG with action-taking tools, and to persist the vector store locally so documents are only embedded once.

The build has two scripts. The first creates a local Chroma vector database: it loads PDF and text meeting notes with LangChain's directory loader, splits them into 1,000-character chunks, embeds them with an open-source Hugging Face model, and saves everything to a chroma_db folder (a SQLite file plus supporting data). The second script is the Asana agent, which already had tools for creating projects and updating tasks bound to an OpenAI or Anthropic chat model. Cole adds a document-query tool that loads the persisted Chroma instance and runs a cosine-similarity search returning the top three matching chunks; the tool's docstring tells the LLM when and how to use it, so retrieval joins the same tool-calling loop as the Asana actions.

The payoff is a Streamlit chatbot where a single prompt can retrieve an answer from the notes (what Emily proposed for the wellness program) and turn it into a new Asana project with tasks. Cole argues the real value is not just time saved per meeting but reduced friction: pulling action items manually is annoying and often skipped, so offloading that grunt work keeps you more organized over time. He closes by teasing a follow-up on moving documents to Google Drive and the vector database to the cloud for a production deployment.

key_moments.note: Three flaws in the prior RAG-only build: always retrieves, no other tools, rebuilds DB every run
Script 1: create a local Chroma vector database and load meeting notes into it
Chunking documents at 1,000 characters per chunk
Embedding with an open-source Hugging Face model and persisting Chroma to disk
Script 2: add RAG as a tool to the existing Asana tool-calling agent
Query tool does a top-3 cosine similarity search over the persisted Chroma DB
Live test: retrieve Emily's wellness program proposal from the notes
Chain RAG into action: create an Asana project and tasks from the retrieved answer

sources.title: AI is TOO EXPENSIVE if You Don't Do This
sources.thesis: Instead of paying for one expensive LLM to handle every request, put a cheap router model in front that classifies each request's complexity and dispatches simple requests to a cheap model and only the rare complex ones to the expensive model, cutting AI costs by 90% or more.
sources.summary:
Cole argues that production AI applications get very expensive because most teams pick a single model powerful (and costly) enough to handle their hardest requests, even though those complex requests are only 5-10% of traffic. Paying premium prices for the 90-95% of requests that a cheap model could handle just fine is the core waste.

The fix is an AI router: a first-step LLM call that outputs a simple yes/no complexity judgment. Simple requests route to a cheap model, complex ones route to the expensive model, so both get a good answer but the bill is a fraction of what it would be. The router itself can run on a cheap model, and even paying for that extra classification step is pennies compared to sending everything to the premium model. The hardest part is writing the router prompt with specific, agent-tailored rules for what counts as complicated.

He demonstrates by enhancing a GPT-4o-powered Asana task-management agent to also use Llama 3 70B via Groq (roughly 11% of GPT-4o's input cost and 5% of its output cost). A helper pulls recent chat history into the router prompt for context, a decide-model function returns 'cheap' or 'expensive', and the prompt-AI function instantiates ChatGroq or ChatOpenAI accordingly. Live tests show greetings and single lookups going to the cheap model, while multi-task creation requests escalate to the expensive one; when routing is off, you just refine the router prompt.

key_moments.note: Promise: save 90% or more on AI costs by not using a single model for everything
Only 5-10% of agent requests are truly complex; the rest are simple
Introduces the AI router pattern via a diagram from a prior video
Router outputs yes/no complexity using custom prompt rules, then routes to cheap or expensive model
Code walkthrough begins on a GPT-4o Asana task agent from the AI Agents Master Class
Adds Llama 3 70B via Groq (~11% input / ~5% output cost of GPT-4o), function-calling fine-tune
Helper pulls recent chat history to give the router context for its decision
Groq/Llama 3 70B used as the router itself because it's so cheap
prompt-AI gains a cheap/expensive parameter selecting ChatGroq vs ChatOpenAI
Live tests: greetings and single lookups go cheap, multi-task creation escalates to expensive
Wrap-up: the hardest part is a good router prompt; the rest of the code is easy

sources.title: The Most Value Packed RAG Guide on YouTube (Feat. Llama 3.1 405B!)
sources.thesis: RAG works by chunking documents into embeddings, matching a user's question to the most similar chunks via vector math, and feeding those chunks to an LLM as context â€” and you can build a working local-document chatbot on Llama 3.1 405B from scratch in minutes.
sources.summary:
This is a concise, beginner-oriented RAG (retrieval augmented generation) tutorial. Cole first explains the mental model: instead of the LLM answering a question directly, the question is routed to a knowledge database where vector mathematics matches it against the most relevant stored knowledge, which is then handed to the LLM as extra context. He walks through the ingestion side â€” every document is split into chunks (100â€“1000 characters), each chunk is turned into a vector and stored as an embedding in a vector database â€” and the retrieval side â€” the user's question is embedded with the same function, compared via cosine similarity, and the top-K most similar chunks (e.g. five) are injected into the prompt.

The back half is a from-scratch build of a Streamlit chatbot that talks to local PDF and text documents. Cole uses LangChain (directory loader + text splitter), a Hugging Face inference endpoint to access Llama 3.1 405B (with commented-out code to run Llama locally instead), an open-source Hugging Face embedding model, and Chroma as an in-memory local vector database. He defines functions to load and chunk documents, build the Chroma store, run a similarity search returning the five most relevant chunks, and prompt the LLM by replacing the last user message with a context-augmented version. He teases that a future video will add tool calling so an agent decides when to invoke RAG rather than doing it every time.

He demonstrates the finished app against a folder of made-up meeting notes (two PDFs, three text files), asking questions like what wellness programs Emily proposed and what was discussed in a specific meeting, showing the system correctly retrieves and concisely summarizes the right information. He frames this as a baseline foundation, noting RAG can be optimized and extended much further in later videos.

key_moments.note: Basic definition of RAG: question routed to knowledge DB, vector math matches relevant knowledge, fed to LLM as context
Ingestion: split documents into chunks, turn chunks into vectors stored as embeddings
Cosine similarity to measure how similar a user question is to a chunk
Retrieval: embed the question with the same function, return top-K similar chunks into the prompt
Transition to building a from-scratch Streamlit chatbot for local docs
Imports: LangChain, Streamlit, Hugging Face for Llama 3.1 405B
Hugging Face inference endpoint vs commented-out code to run Llama locally
LangChain directory loader loads all PDF and text documents from a folder
Chroma as the in-memory local vector database
Query function uses Chroma similarity search to return five most relevant chunks
Prompting: replace the last user message with a context+question augmented message
Live demo querying local meeting-notes documents, correct answers returned

sources.title: AI Agents: The Future of AI or a Passing Fad?
sources.thesis: AI agents are not hype but a practical necessity, because they solve the five biggest structural problems of raw LLMs: isolation, prompt bloat, hallucination, cost, and lack of state persistence.
sources.summary:
Cole defines an AI agent in the simplest terms: a large language model (Claude, GPT, or Llama) that has been given the ability to interact with external services or with other AI models and to maintain persistent state, letting it accomplish far more than an isolated chatbot. He argues that the value of agents is best understood by looking at the specific weaknesses of base LLMs that agents fix, and he grounds every point in real applications he has built for businesses rather than theory.

He walks through five problems and their agentic solutions. (1) Isolation: a base LLM only answers from training data, so a lead-nurturing app uses RAG for FAQs plus calendar tools to actually check availability and book meetings, which also prevents the model from falsely claiming it took an action. (2) Prompt bloat: cramming every tool into one prompt triggers the needle-in-a-haystack problem, so a router agent classifies the request and forwards it to a specialized agent (Asana, Google Drive, Gmail, vector DB) whose prompt is small and focused. (3) Hallucination: a second model with a separate evaluation prompt self-reflects on the first model's output, checking for made-up info, malformed output, and missing/erroneous tool calls, and loops back for regeneration with feedback if the answer is bad.

(4) Cost: a router judges request complexity and routes the easy 95% to a cheap model (Llama 3) while reserving an expensive model (GPT-4o) for the hard 5%, cutting spend without hurting quality. (5) State persistence: chat history degrades as it grows, so a workout-generator app stores each workout in a database as structured persistent state, feeding a fresh clean piece to each prompt and making the data easy to render in the UI, with RAG teased as the path to true long-term memory. His conclusion: nearly any useful real-world AI application only works because it leverages agents.

key_moments.note: Simplest definition of an AI agent: an LLM that interacts with external services or other models and maintains persistent state
Problem 1 - LLMs are isolated; lead-nurturing app uses RAG + calendar tools to act in the real world
Problem 2 - prompt bloat / needle-in-a-haystack; solved with a router forwarding to specialized agents
Problem 3 - hallucination; solved with a second self-reflection/evaluation model in a regeneration loop
Problem 4 - cost; a router routes simple requests to a cheap model (Llama 3) and hard ones to an expensive model (GPT-4o)
Problem 5 - state persistence; workout-generator app stores structured state in a database instead of relying on chat history
Conclusion: almost any useful real-world AI application is only useful because it leverages agents

sources.title: New Llama 3 Model BEATS GPT and Claude with Function Calling!?
sources.thesis: Groq's fine-tuned Llama 3 models top the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard, so an open-source model you can run locally is now competitive with GPT and Claude for driving AI agents.
sources.summary:
Cole reacts to Groq's release of Llama 3 models specifically tuned for function calling, which claimed the top spots on the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard. The 70B version is ranked #1 at ~90% accuracy and the 8B version is #3, beating every GPT model and every Claude model except 3.5 Sonnet. Cole frames this as a historic first: the best function-calling model is now open source rather than proprietary.

To test the claim beyond the benchmark, he runs the same task-management AI agent (built in LangChain with a Streamlit UI, controlling Asana) under two models: GPT-4o first, then Groq's Llama 3 70B. GPT-4o handles a complex multi-tool request flawlessly (research 10 steps, create an Asana project, add each step as a task due Friday, then complete/delete/add tasks). Swapping to Groq requires only importing ChatGroq from LangChain and changing the model instantiation, everything else stays the same.

Groq's Llama 3 completes the same workflow but with more friction: it is dramatically faster per response, yet it asks the user to resolve the actual date for 'Friday', fails to look up task IDs on its own, and occasionally updates the wrong task. Cole concludes it is 'almost as good' as GPT, attributing the gap mostly to GPT's better handling of many tokens, and calls near-parity from a local open-source model a big win for accessible, transparent AI.

key_moments.note: Claim: for the first time the best function-calling LLM is an open-source model you can run locally
Groq's 70B Llama 3 is #1 (~90%) and 8B is #3 on the Berkeley Function Calling Leaderboard
Sets up a head-to-head test using his Asana task-management agent, GPT-4o first
GPT-4o completes the complex multi-tool Asana workflow flawlessly
Shows the code change to switch to Groq: import ChatGroq, swap the model
Groq Llama 3 is much faster but asks the user to confirm the date for Friday
Groq model fails to look up task IDs itself and updates the wrong task
Verdict: almost as good as GPT, a huge victory for open-source / local models

sources.title: Build a Hilarious AI Game from SCRATCH in 10 Minutes
sources.thesis: You can build a genuinely fun, useful AI app fast by pairing a single well-structured LLM prompt with schema validation and a simple state machine in a Streamlit chat UI.
sources.summary:
Cole builds an AI Mad Libs chatbot from an empty skeleton in about five minutes. The core idea is to prompt GPT (recommended: gpt-4o) exactly once to generate an entire Mad Lib, returning JSON with two fields: the story text and a list of blanks to fill. That single-prompt design keeps the app fast and cheap because there is no second call needed to render the finished story to the user.

The reliability of the app comes from enforcing a JSON schema with LangChain output parsers. A class defines the expected shape (a string for text and a list of strings for blanks), and the parser validates the model's response against it, throwing an error and failing the script if the output does not match. Cole stresses this as the antidote to LLM hallucination and 'trash output': if parsing succeeds you are guaranteed a well-formed object you can safely key off of for the rest of the app. He also chains prompt -> chat model -> output parser as a LangChain chain.

The app flow is driven by a two-state machine stored in Streamlit session state: 'choosing theme' (generate the Mad Lib) and 'choosing words' (fill blanks one at a time, then print the completed story and reset to choosing theme for an infinite loop). A small helper function mimics token streaming since real OpenAI streaming can't be used when the response must first be parsed as JSON. Cole then demos it live with an 'AI taking over the world' theme.

key_moments.note: Intro: pitch for an AI Mad Libs chatbot that generates a themed Mad Lib and fills blanks one at a time
Load environment variables and OpenAI API key; recommends gpt-4o with a default fallback
The single generation prompt: produce the whole Mad Lib and blanks in one call with a defined JSON schema
Enforcing the JSON schema via LangChain output parsers to validate the LLM response
Helper function to simulate streaming text to the user since real streaming can't be used with JSON parsing
Streamlit session state: messages list plus a state of 'choosing theme' or 'choosing words'
Schema validation catches hallucinated 'trash output' so a passing parse guarantees usable JSON
Building the LangChain chain: prompt -> GPT -> JSON output parser, invoked with the user's theme
Word-filling logic: replace the first blank with the bolded user word, advance or finish
Live demo of the finished Streamlit Mad Libs bot with the theme 'AI taking over the world'

sources.title: I FINALLY Built an AI Agent to Organize my Life - AI Agents Masterclass #4
sources.thesis: By giving an LLM a set of well-documented Python functions as tools, you can build a conversational agent that fully manages your Asana workspace (projects and tasks) from natural language.
sources.summary:
This masterclass installment turns the simple task-creating chatbot from the prior video into a full task-management AI agent wired into Asana. Cole demos the finished product first: the agent researches a topic, then from a single natural-language prompt creates a new Asana project, generates a task per item, and assigns due dates by reasoning about the current date. It can also mark tasks complete, delete tasks, and update due dates from conversational, non-explicit requests, returning links to everything it touches.

The build walks through adding new tools alongside the existing create-task function: get projects, create project, get tasks, update task, and delete task. Each is a plain Python function that calls the Asana SDK and returns either the response or an error message as a string. The critical piece is each function's docstring, which tells the agent when to use the tool, what parameters to pass (including how to shape a generic 'data' dictionary), and exactly what the response will contain. Cole stresses that these docstrings are the most important part of the prompt, because a poorly described tool gets used at the wrong times or ignored when needed.

Adding tools is nearly free: functions are registered in an available-functions mapping that is dynamically fetched and bound into the chatbot, so no other code changes. A final tweak to the system prompt instructs the agent never to surface internal IDs to the user. The video closes teasing the next installment on RAG, where a recorded meeting will be transcribed via Google Drive and its action items turned into Asana tasks.

key_moments.note: Overview of everything the Asana chatbot can do: create/search projects, create/delete/update/complete tasks
Demo: research a list, then one prompt creates a project and a task per item with computed due dates
Marking a task complete from implicit natural language ('I have learned python now')
Code walkthrough begins; workspace ID replaces single project ID
Building the new tools: get projects, create project, get tasks, update task, delete task
Registering all tools in the available-functions mapping; dynamically bound to the chatbot
Docstrings are the most important part of the prompt for correct tool use
System prompt tweak: never expose internal IDs to the user
Teaser for the next video on RAG: meeting recording to Google Drive transcript to Asana action items

sources.title: Function Calling with ANY LLM for Local AI Agents (Feat. LangChain, HuggingFace, and Llama 3)
sources.thesis: You can add function/tool calling to any local LLM that lacks it natively by injecting tool descriptions into the system prompt and forcing the model to return a validated JSON schema of tool calls.
sources.summary:
Cole demonstrates how to implement custom function calling from scratch so that lightweight, local models like Meta's Llama 3 or Microsoft's Phi, which don't ship with native function calling like GPT and Claude, can still act as true AI agents. The trick is entirely prompt-and-parsing based: build a mapping of function names to Python function objects, auto-generate tool descriptions from each function's docstring (name, arguments, types, return value), and inject that list plus a strict JSON schema into the system prompt so the model knows when and how to call each tool.

The model is required to respond in a JSON object with two keys: a `tool_calls` array (each with a name and arguments) and a `content` string for raw user-facing text. LangChain's JSON output parser validates the response against the schema; if the model produces malformed output, the code recurses to retry with a bounded retry count so it never loops forever. Because local models hallucinate more, the code also tracks already-invoked tools to prevent duplicate calls. Tool results are fed back as internal 'thought' messages so the model can compose a final natural-language reply.

The demo app is built with Streamlit and LangChain, pulls the model from HuggingFace (via a serverless endpoint, swappable for a fully local HuggingFace pipeline or Ollama), and uses a single Asana 'create task' tool to prove real external-service interaction. Cole shows Llama 3 correctly creating an Asana task from the message 'I need to mow the lawn by Saturday,' while warning that local models are weaker than GPT/Claude and this custom approach will surface more hallucinations.

key_moments.note: Function calling is what turns LLMs into agents that interact with external services
Problem framed: local models like Llama and Phi lack built-in function calling
Asana chosen as the external service; single create-task tool for demo
Tool descriptions built by pulling docstrings from Python functions
System prompt must force JSON output so tool calls are parseable
Recursion/retry logic when the model doesn't conform to the schema
Deduplication of tool calls to guard against local-model hallucination
Live demo: Llama 3 creates the 'mow the lawn' Asana task
Warning: local models hallucinate more and may output malformed JSON

sources.title: AI Agents Just Got Sexy - AI Agents Masterclass #3
sources.thesis: You can wrap an existing tool-calling AI agent in a beautiful, deployable chat UI in about five minutes using Streamlit, then add typewriter-style streaming with Python generators.
sources.summary:
In the third AI Agents Masterclass video, Cole Medin takes the Asana-integrated LangChain agent built in prior videos and puts a real user interface around it using Streamlit, arguing that many people either ship agents with an ugly terminal interface or waste weeks hand-building React/HTML front ends when they don't need to. Because Streamlit re-runs the entire Python script on every UI change, the core lesson is managing chat history and user input through Streamlit's session state, guarded by an initialization check so the message history isn't reset on every rerun. The main function loops over session-state messages, renders each with st.chat_message + st.markdown, and reacts to st.chat_input, updating both the UI and the session state together to keep them consistent.

The second half adds streaming so responses type out chunk-by-chunk instead of appearing all at once. Cole explains that streaming is trivial for a plain chatbot (return the stream and hand it to Streamlit), but trickier with tool calling because the agent may call the LLM twice: once to decide on a tool, and again after the tool result. The solution is Python generators: instead of returning the stream (which would end the function before the tool runs), the prompt function yields each chunk while continuing on to invoke the tool and produce a second stream, whose chunks are also yielded. Streamlit's st.write_stream consumes the generator, and the full response is reassembled manually to store in session state.

The result is a clean, deployable web chatbot that creates real Asana tasks, renders returned links as hyperlinks via markdown, and streams responses live thanks to Streamlit's hot reloading. Cole frames it as the UI foundation for all future masterclass projects, previewing a next video that expands the agent into a full task manager.

key_moments.note: Problem framing: agents with ugly or over-engineered UIs; promise of a simple beautiful interface
Import Streamlit; the LLM-calling functions don't need to change, only main()
Add a title component with st.title
Initialize chat history in session state, seeded with a system message + current date
Key insight: Streamlit re-runs the whole script on every UI change, so guard state init with an if-check
Loop over session-state messages and render each with st.chat_message + st.markdown
React to st.chat_input; update both the UI and session state together
Run with 'streamlit run agent_with_ui.py'; browser tab opens automatically
Live demo: create an Asana task from chat; returned link renders as a hyperlink
Bonus section: adding typewriter-style streaming
Why streaming + tool calling is tricky: two LLM calls, can't return early
Use Python generators (yield) to stream chunks while still invoking the tool
st.write_stream renders the generator; rebuild the full message for session state
Streamlit hot reloading applies code changes without restarting
Streaming demo, including a tool call that creates a task after a warning

sources.title: I Made a Custom AI Chatbot Site in 47 Lines of Code
sources.thesis: You can build a fully-functional GPT-powered custom chatbot with a real web UI in just 47 lines of Python using Streamlit and LangChain, which is the foundation for building AI agents that go beyond what ChatGPT can do.
sources.summary:
Cole walks through building a custom GPT-powered chatbot with a complete user interface in only 47 lines of Python. He opens by answering the obvious objection: why build a custom chatbot instead of just using the ChatGPT website? The answer is power and extensibility. A custom chatbot can interact with external services, third-party libraries, and API endpoints to do things ChatGPT cannot, like scheduling appointments, managing tasks, or checking calendar availability. This is the entry point into building AI agents.

The build uses Streamlit for the UI and LangChain for talking to the LLM (GPT-4o by default, set via an environment variable). He covers the core mechanics: adding UI components by calling `st.<component>` (title, chat_message, chat_input), seeding the conversation with a system message that sets context (including the current date, which many models lack), and the critical Streamlit behavior that the entire Python script reruns on every UI state change. Because of this rerun model, message history must be persisted in Streamlit's session state and guarded with an `if messages not in session_state` check so it isn't reset on every rerun.

When the user submits a prompt, the app appends it to both the UI and the session state, invokes the LLM with the full message history, and streams the response back in typewriter style via Streamlit's stream writer. The AI's response is then also saved to session state. Cole notes the demo chatbot is basic and not yet worth using over ChatGPT, but stresses the real value: now that it is custom-coded, it can be extended into an AI agent that interacts with a calendar, tasks, Discord, or Slack.

key_moments.note: Intro: build a GPT-powered custom chatbot UI in 47 lines of Python
Why build a custom chatbot instead of using the ChatGPT website â€” power and external integrations
Imports, requirements.txt, and .env.example for OpenAI auth + model selection
Model defaults to GPT-4o via LLM_MODEL env var
Initializing the system message in Streamlit session state
Streamlit reruns the entire script on every UI state change â€” why the session-state guard matters
chat_input component captures the user prompt
Create OpenAI chatbot instance and stream the response into the UI
Running the app and demoing it live in the browser with typewriter streaming
Next step: extend the custom chatbot into an AI agent that touches calendar, tasks, Discord, Slack

sources.title: The Secret Sauce for SIMPLE + POWERFUL AI Apps - AI Agents Masterclass #2
sources.thesis: LangChain dramatically simplifies building tool-using AI agents by replacing the raw GPT SDK's verbose function-calling boilerplate with docstring-driven tool decorators and message abstractions, and makes agents model-agnostic so you can swap between GPT and Claude in a single line.
sources.summary:
Building on the prior masterclass agent that used the raw GPT SDK, Cole refactors it into a LangChain agent. LangChain provides a suite of tools (chat memory, output parsers, dynamic prompts, message types) so you don't have to build the plumbing of an AI app yourself. The biggest simplification is tool calling: instead of hand-writing a large JSON object describing a function's name, description, and parameters, you decorate the Python function with LangChain's @tool decorator. LangChain reads the function's docstring and signature to tell the agent when to call the tool and what arguments to pass, so the messy schema object can be deleted entirely.

The refactor also swaps raw JSON messages for LangChain's SystemMessage, HumanMessage, AIMessage, and ToolMessage objects, and uses ChatOpenAI with bind_tools() to attach the tool list to the model. The agent loop stays structurally the same (invoke the model, check for tool calls, run the mapped function, append the tool result, invoke again for the final human-facing response) but is far less bloated. The example task-creation flow (an Asana 'create task' tool) works identically to before.

The payoff is provider independence. Because different model providers return tool calls in different formats (Anthropic vs OpenAI have distinct shapes), combining SDKs directly would take real work. LangChain normalizes this, so adding Claude is just importing ChatAnthropic and branching on an env var: if the model name contains 'GPT' use ChatOpenAI, otherwise use ChatAnthropic. Cole demonstrates the same agent creating tasks under both GPT-4o and Claude 3.5 Sonnet, noting the models sometimes phrase responses differently. He frames LangChain as the groundwork for every future video in the masterclass, including multi-agent systems with LangGraph.

key_moments.note: Intro: build powerful AI apps simply with AI agents and LangChain
Plan: take the prior GPT-SDK agent and convert it to a LangChain agent
Swap raw JSON messages for LangChain SystemMessage/HumanMessage/AIMessage objects
Add the @tool decorator so LangChain reads the docstring to know when to call the function
Delete the messy function-schema JSON object entirely â€” the docstring replaces it
Use ChatOpenAI + bind_tools() and invoke the tool-augmented model
Test the LangChain agent: it creates a 'bake cookies' Asana task due Sunday
Why LangChain matters: providers return tool calls in different formats; LangChain normalizes them
Add Claude: import ChatAnthropic and branch on the model env var in one line
Run the same agent on Claude 3.5 Sonnet; it creates 'mow the lawn' task identically

sources.title: The ONLY AI Developer Roadmap You Need in 2024
sources.thesis: A 10-step roadmap to go from zero to hero as an AI developer: master fundamentals fast, don't over-invest in prompt engineering or custom model training, and spend most of your time on LLM app-building, RAG, and AI agents.
sources.summary:
Cole lays out a sequential 10-step learning path for becoming an AI developer in 2024. The early steps are foundational and meant to be moved through quickly: master Python (functions, OOP, basics â€” don't spend six months), learn how AI models and LLMs are trained and where they fail, and pick up prompt engineering (a few hours is enough â€” he explicitly pushes back on gurus who frame prompt engineering as a $300k career). Deep learning / ML / NLP is marked optional, because GPT and Claude are powerful enough for most use cases; you only need to train custom models for specialized tasks (his example: a builder.io-style figma-to-code model where general GPT failed).

The middle steps are where real value accrues. Step five is building basic projects directly against the OpenAI and Anthropic SDKs in Python (chatbots, summarizers, idea generators) and, critically, learning to read SDK documentation instead of leaning on tutorials. Step six is LangChain, his favorite tool â€” a suite of Python libraries that handle chat memory, chains, output parsing, and prompt templating so you build faster and with fewer errors. Step seven is RAG (retrieval augmented generation): augmenting an LLM with external knowledge via vector databases and embeddings so the model has the context it needs.

Step eight, AI agents, is where he spends most of his own time and says takes the most effort: the LangChain suite (LangGraph for multi-agent infrastructure, LangServe for deployment, LangSmith for observability) plus LLM function calling to let agents interact with databases, APIs, and services, plus cloud deployment. Step nine is building front ends (HTML/CSS/JS, React/Next.js, the Vercel AI SDK) so real users can access the app. Step ten is a grab-bag of advanced, use-case-dependent topics: fine-tuning, multimodal models, explainable AI, mixture of agents, and app development for mobile.

key_moments.note: Intro: condensing years of AI learning into a zero-to-hero roadmap
Step 1 â€” Master Python (basics, don't over-invest)
Step 2 â€” Basics of AI and LLMs (how they're trained, pitfalls)
Step 3 â€” Prompt engineering (a few hours is enough; pushback on gurus)
Step 4 â€” Deep learning / ML / NLP marked as OPTIONAL
builder.io figma-to-code example: when a custom model is worth it
Step 5 â€” Build basic projects on OpenAI/Anthropic SDKs; learn to read docs
Step 6 â€” LangChain, his favorite AI tool
Step 7 â€” RAG, vector databases and embeddings
Step 8 â€” AI agents: LangGraph/LangServe/LangSmith + function calling + deploy
Step 9 â€” Front ends (React/Next.js, Vercel AI SDK)
Step 10 â€” Advanced use-case topics: fine-tuning, multimodal, mixture of agents

sources.title: Developers, THIS is How you Stay Relevant with AI - AI Agents Masterclass #1
sources.thesis: AI agents â€” LLMs given the ability to interact with the outside world through tool calls â€” are the real way to unleash AI's power, and you can build a functional one from scratch in a few minutes.
sources.summary:
This is the first episode of Cole's AI Agents Masterclass, arguing that developers who want to stay relevant must focus on AI agents, not just chatbots. He defines an agent simply as a large language model given the ability to interact with the outside world (draft emails, create tasks, update a CRM), and frames tool calling as the thing that turns a plain GPT chatbot into an agent.

The bulk of the video is a from-scratch Python build: a command-line GPT chatbot wired to the Asana API so it can create real tasks. Cole walks through the OpenAI client setup, environment variables for secrets, the message list with its three roles (system, user, assistant), a forever-loop that accumulates chat history, a create_asana_task function, and â€” critically â€” the tool schema (a JSON definition with a description telling the model when to call the function and a parameters block telling it what to pass). He shows how the model parses natural language into structured arguments (task name, a correctly-formatted due date), returns tool_calls in its response, and how the code executes the function and feeds the result back so the model can write a final human-facing reply with the task link.

The demo confirms the loop: a normal question gets a normal answer, but 'create a task to make a thumbnail, due Wednesday' triggers the tool call and the task appears in Asana. Cole closes by noting the next episode will show an easier, more powerful way to build the same thing.

key_moments.note: Thesis: developers must focus on AI agents, defined as an LLM able to interact with the outside world
Project setup: skeleton main function for a GPT chatbot that creates Asana tasks
Imports, environment variables (.env) for API keys, loading them with dotenv
Creating the OpenAI client and the Asana tasks API client

Forever loop reading user input and building up chat history

prompt_ai function: client.chat.completions.create with model, messages, tools
The tool schema â€” description (when to call) + parameters (what to pass), including date format
Parsing the GPT response: choices[0].message and the tool_calls attribute
Mapping tool-call names to Python functions, invoking with **args, appending result to messages
Re-prompting the AI with the function result so it writes the final user reply
Live demo: normal question vs. a task request that triggers the tool call and returns a task link
The created task confirmed in Asana; teaser that the next episode makes it simpler and more powerful

sources.title: Introducing Trainer's Ally - Nvidia AI Agents Competition Submission
sources.thesis: Trainer's Ally is an AI agent app (built on Nvidia + LangChain) that lets personal trainers co-create fully customized multi-day client workouts in minutes through an iterative human-in-the-loop generate-revise-continue flow.
sources.summary:
This short demo introduces Trainer's Ally, an AI agents application submitted to the Nvidia AI Agents competition and powered by Nvidia and LangChain technologies. Its premise is that personal trainers can work alongside AI to produce highly customized client workouts in minutes rather than by hand. The user first supplies criteria such as the client's available gym equipment and their fitness goals, then clicks 'generate workouts' to produce the plan day by day.

The core of the experience is an iterative human-in-the-loop loop. For each generated day the trainer can accept it as-is, swap in AI-provided alternatives, or type free-form natural-language feedback (e.g. adjustments) that the agent uses to revise that day's workout before moving on. The trainer repeats this across the week, choosing at each step how much to intervene, until the final multi-day program is complete.

The workflow ends with the finished week of workouts, which the trainer can export into an existing personal-training app (Everfit is mentioned). The video is a product walkthrough rather than a technical teardown, so it demonstrates the application pattern of a domain-specific AI agent with a lightweight review/revision UI more than any underlying architecture.

key_moments.note: Intro: Trainer's Ally, an AI agents app powered by Nvidia and LangChain for personal trainers
Supply workout criteria: client's gym equipment access and fitness goals
Click 'generate workouts'; first day generated with option to pick alternatives or continue
Free-form text feedback revises the workout for the next day
Continue without revising, or just swap AI-provided alternatives per day
Final week of workouts produced; export into a training app like Everfit

sources.title: I Will Win the Nvidia AI Agents Developer Contest
sources.thesis: Cole announces he will win the Nvidia/LangChain AI agents developer contest by building an agentic, human-in-the-loop application that helps personal trainers generate personalized weekly workouts for their clients.
sources.summary:
In this early announcement video, Cole introduces the Nvidia and LangChain Generative AI Agents Developer Contest and his plan to win it by building an AI agent application for the personal training industry. His wife is a personal trainer who spends 30-60+ minutes per client each week building workouts, so Cole frames AI as an opportunity to save that time while keeping workouts highly personalized. He explains the contest premise (build an AI agent project using Nvidia and LangChain technologies) and its judging criteria: real-world usefulness, quality of technology integration, and quality of the submission (a 45-90 second demo plus documentation).

Cole defines an AI agent as a large language model augmented with tools to interact with the outside world (booking calendars, querying databases), and notes that when multiple agents work together toward one goal you can do far more than a bare LLM. His application takes client criteria (weight, goals, equipment access, number and length of workouts) and generates the week one day at a time: it produces a day's workout, returns it to the trainer for feedback and alternatives, revises, then moves to the next day in a loop until the full week is planned. This human-in-the-loop design is deliberate. Cole argues AI is not good enough to replace personal trainers, especially on accountability, so the goal is to augment trainers rather than replace them (contrasting with individual-focused apps like Dr. Muscle).

He reports the backend agent application already works (prompting tuned with his wife's feedback, currently running on Groq with Llama), with the remaining work being to integrate Nvidia technologies and build a front end. He commits to livestreaming his build every day until the June 17 submission deadline to push himself and bring viewers along.

key_moments.note: Introduces the Nvidia + LangChain Generative AI Agents Developer Contest
Explains why he's building for personal trainers (his wife) and the time cost of manual workout planning
Contest premise and judging criteria (usefulness, tech integration, submission quality)
Defines an AI agent as an LLM augmented with tools; agents working together
Walks through the day-by-day workout generation loop with trainer feedback
Argues AI should augment, not replace, personal trainers (accountability)
Current stack: Groq + Llama; next steps are Nvidia integration and a front end
Commits to livestreaming the build daily until the June 17 deadline

sources.title: ChatGPT Teaches the Secrets to Financial Freedom!?
sources.thesis: You can turn any popular book's author into a personalized coach by giving ChatGPT the book's philosophy plus your full life context, then asking for a concrete tailored plan.
sources.summary:
In this early (2023) ChatGPT-prompting video, Cole demonstrates a technique for getting personalized financial advice from GPT-4: take a well-known book (here, Rich Dad Poor Dad) and prompt the model to act as a financial advisor who follows that book's author. Because GPT is already trained on popular finance books, you only need to name the book and add a sentence of framing for the model to adopt its philosophy, which solves the core limitation of books, that generic advice is never tailored to your unique situation.

The prompt is built as a three-step structure. First, establish context: the book/author whose advice you want to follow. Second, dump your full personal situation, age, location, profession, income, expenses, living situation, current investments, skills, assets, and life goals, so the advice is personalized to you. Third, deliver the 'ask': request concrete deliverables like a 1/5/10/20/30-year plan plus practical starting points and action steps, and instruct the model to give its opinion directly rather than hedging about subjectivity.

Cole walks through GPT's generated multi-horizon plan (monetizing digital real estate, building an emergency fund, diversifying into real estate per Kiyosaki's philosophy, learning sales/marketing/tax skills) and shows how you can drill down on any part with follow-up prompts. He frames this as more powerful than robo-advisors like Vanguard's because you can feed the model exactly the context you think it needs.

key_moments.note: Framing: books give great financial advice but aren't personalized to your unique situation
The core idea: turn a book's author (e.g., Rich Dad Poor Dad) into your personal finance coach via GPT
Prompt part 1: set the model as a financial advisor following a chosen book/author, add a sentence of context
GPT already knows popular finance books from training, so minimal book context is needed
Prompt part 2: supply full personal context (age, location, profession, income, expenses, investments, skills, assets, goals)
Prompt part 3: the ask, request 1/5/10/20/30-year plans plus starting points and action steps, tell it to opine directly
Walkthrough of GPT's multi-horizon plan (monetize digital real estate, emergency fund, real estate, skills, taxes)
Drill down with follow-up prompts on any part (e.g., real estate investing or monetizing the Twitter account)
Recap of the three-step process: book context, your info, the ask

sources.title: 3 MOST Important Prompt Engineering Techniques you NEED to know!
sources.thesis: Effective prompt engineering comes down to three simple, high-leverage techniques (self-critique, role personification, and few-shot examples) rather than the dozens of overwrought methods people obsess over.
sources.summary:
Cole frames prompt engineering as simply crafting your questions so the AI gives the answer you actually want, and argues that most people fall into one of two traps: knowing too little (and getting disappointing results) or over-researching dozens of techniques until writing a prompt takes longer than the answer is worth. His pitch is to keep it simple and rely on three techniques that cover nearly every use case.

The three techniques are: (1) have the AI criticize its own output, personifying it as a critic with specific quality requirements, then rewrite based on that critique; (2) personify the AI into a role that freely gives opinions, which overrides the model's tendency to punt with 'it's subjective' and forces a direct answer; and (3) provide question/answer examples before your real question when you need a specific, parseable output format or a particular voice/tone. He identifies role personification as the overarching concept wrapping all of prompt engineering.

He also pushes back on commonly recommended techniques that add little value, singling out 'think step by step' as unnecessary with strong models like GPT-4 that already reason that way by default. His broader point: many shared prompt templates only work on the cherry-picked examples their authors demo, so don't waste time implementing techniques that don't reliably improve the output.

key_moments.note: Defines prompt engineering as crafting questions to get the answer you want; bad prompts give bad answers
Technique 1: have the AI criticize itself, personified as a tweet critic with specific requirements
Technique 2: personify the AI as a specialist who gives opinions to defeat 'it's subjective' answers (car wrap color example)
Technique 3: provide question/answer examples to force a specific, parseable output format (math equation example)
Examples also help match a specific voice/tone for creative content like tweets
Overrated technique: telling GPT-4 to 'think step by step' is unnecessary since it already does
Recap of all three techniques

sources.title: ChatGPT Users BE AWARE of this Major Weakness
sources.thesis: The single biggest weakness of GPT and similar AI models is the training cutoff, which freezes their knowledge at a point in time and causes them to silently produce outdated answers, but fine-tuning, search plugins, and faster/cheaper model training are closing that gap.
sources.summary:
Cole explains that despite the hype around GPT, its greatest weakness is the training cutoff: the model is trained on data up to a fixed date (September 2021 for the GPT of this era) and knows nothing after it. You can discover the cutoff by simply asking the model. This matters most for current events and for coding, where libraries and tools change constantly. He demonstrates with a Discord bot: ChatGPT writes working code but admits it is using discord.js version 12 (not the current version 14), and cannot help with the newest version because it was released after the cutoff. Worse, the model often does not warn you that its answer is outdated because it has no way of knowing whether a newer version exists.

He walks through why relying on outdated library code is dangerous: migrating from discord.js 13 to 14 involves many breaking changes, so it can be faster to write from the current docs than to fix the model's stale output. He then covers workarounds: fine-tuning your own model on question/answer pairs derived from up-to-date documentation, and using an internet-search plugin to fetch current information.

Finally, Cole argues the training cutoff will shrink over time. Training is expensive and slow because of the billions of parameters involved, but research (he cites Meta's model trained with ~10x fewer parameters) is making models cheaper and faster to train. As parameter counts drop, models could be retrained daily on fresh GitHub and news data, collapsing the cutoff from years to hours or days and eventually removing the need for search plugins or custom knowledge bases at all.

key_moments.note: Defines the training cutoff and shows you can ask GPT directly (answer: September 2021)
Discord bot example: ChatGPT writes working code but admits it uses discord.js v12, not the current v14
Breaking changes between discord.js 13 and 14 mean stale generated code can be slower to fix than starting fresh
Workaround 1: fine-tune your own model on Q/A pairs from up-to-date documentation
Future fix: fewer parameters (cites Meta at 10x less) means faster, cheaper, more frequent retraining
Search plugins as a workaround, but daily-retrained base models could make them unnecessary
The real danger: the model often does not tell you its answer is outdated because it can't know

sources.title: Use ChatGPT in Two Clicks on ANY website with a Custom Chrome Extension
sources.thesis: You can build a simple Chrome extension backed by a small Express API that lets you invoke ChatGPT on any website via a right-click, replacing highlighted or in-box text with the model's response.
sources.summary:
This 2023 tutorial walks through building a Chrome extension ("Chrome GPT") that removes the friction of leaving your current page to use ChatGPT. Instead of navigating to the OpenAI UI, signing in, selecting a model, prompting, copying, and pasting back, you right-click inside any text box (or highlight specific text), click the extension's context-menu option, and it replaces your text with GPT's response in place. Cole demonstrates it on Twitter, a YouTube comment box, and an online code editor.

The extension's anatomy is three parts. manifest.json is the metadata backbone declaring the manifest version, name, permissions, icons, a background service worker, and content scripts. background.js registers a right-click context-menu item and, on click, sends a message to content.js. content.js does the real work: it reads the active element's text (or the highlighted selection), shows a loading cursor, POSTs the prompt to a local API, then writes GPT's reply back into the text box (handling both textarea/input elements and contentEditable via ranges).

The browser can't safely hold an OpenAI key, so Cole stands up a minimal Express.js server (server.js) that reads the API key and org from a .env file, calls OpenAI's chat completion with gpt-3.5-turbo, trims leading whitespace, and returns the reply. He loads the unpacked extension via chrome://extensions, notes you must refresh pages to pick up a reloaded extension, and closes by outlining how to productionize it: host the API publicly, add auth (e.g. Firebase), and add Stripe for payments, plus richer prompt engineering for use cases like tweet replies or YouTube video summarization.

key_moments.note: The friction problem: constantly switching to the ChatGPT UI to copy answers back to where you're working
Demo goal: a Chrome extension to interact with GPT on any website via right-click
Live demos on Twitter, a YouTube comment box, and an online code editor (highlighting a selection)
Chrome extensions are just HTML/CSS/JS â€” starting with manifest.json as the backbone
Manifest references a background service worker and content scripts
background.js registers the right-click context menu and messages content.js
content.js reads the active/highlighted text, calls the API, and writes the reply back
contentEditable elements must be handled differently using a range
Building a minimal Express.js API with a .env holding the OpenAI key
Server calls OpenAI chat completion with gpt-3.5-turbo on port 3000
Loading the unpacked extension via chrome://extensions and refreshing to reload it
Productionizing: public API, Firebase auth, Stripe payments, richer prompting like YouTube summarization

sources.title: You NEED These 5 Tools For Coding!! (Better Than ChatGPT)
sources.thesis: An early-2023 roundup of five AI coding assistants (GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, Mutable.ai, Sapphire AI, 10Web) that offload the tedious, low-leverage parts of software development so developers can focus on what matters.
sources.summary:
This is one of Cole Medin's earliest videos, a survey of the first wave of AI coding tools in April 2023. The framing is that coding has many tedious, low-leverage pain points (writing tests, adding documentation, remembering syntax) and AI can now automate them, freeing developers to build what actually matters. He walks through five tools and repeatedly draws the same key distinction: some assistants push automatic inline suggestions as you type (GitHub Copilot, Tabnine, paid Mutable.ai), while others require you to manually invoke the AI with a keyboard shortcut (free Mutable.ai, Sapphire AI). He notes the choice is largely preference.

The tools covered are GitHub Copilot (the most popular, ~$10/mo, understands API responses and good code structure), Tabnine (a heavy autocomplete that infers a whole database schema and helper functions from just a name), Mutable.ai (free for manual suggestions, with dedicated documentation-generation features), Sapphire AI (a free, newer, manual-invoke VS Code extension), and 10Web (an outlier: AI-powered WordPress site building, hosting, and page-speed automation rather than in-IDE coding).

Recurring themes are that AI is especially valuable for the tasks developers dread â€” writing tests and adding documentation â€” because those feel like they don't advance the end product. The video is a time capsule of the pre-Copilot-Chat, pre-agent era, and Cole closes by predicting the space will change so fast that a similar video will be worth making within the year.

key_moments.note: Framing: AI removes the low-leverage, tedious details so you can build what matters
Core distinction introduced: automatic inline suggestions vs manual-request assistants
Tool 1: GitHub Copilot â€” most popular, ~$10/mo, understands APIs and good structure
AI for writing tests, the most tedious dev task
Tool 2: Tabnine â€” heavy autocomplete infers a DB schema from just a name
Tool 3: Mutable.ai â€” free manual suggestions, dedicated documentation features
Tool 4: Sapphire AI â€” free, newer, manual-invoke VS Code extension
Tool 5: 10Web â€” AI-powered WordPress site building/hosting/speed

sources.title: ChatGPT is the GIFT that keeps on GIVING - Introducing Auto-GPT
sources.thesis: Auto-GPT wraps GPT-4 in an autonomous loop that decomposes a goal into self-generated steps, executes them with tool use and memory, and self-criticizes along the way â€” an early glimpse of agentic AI.
sources.summary:
Cole introduces Auto-GPT, an open-source experiment that turns GPT-4 from a single-turn chatbot into an autonomous agent: you give it a role and a handful of goals, and it generates its own steps to reach them, executes those steps, and keeps going. He frames this as the 'sparks of AGI' â€” an AI that can define and pursue its own subgoals â€” and walks through the trivially simple setup (Python 3.8+, an OpenAI API key, git clone, rename the .env.template) plus optional add-ons like a Pinecone vector database for long-term memory and ElevenLabs for spoken output.

The core of the video is a live demo where he builds 'Twitter-GPT' and gives it three goals around growing a Twitter account. The agent thinks step by step, prints its thoughts/reasoning/plan, and critically self-criticizes to check its own work before proposing each next action. It browses Twitter, falls back to a Google search when that yields nothing (overcoming GPT's September-2021 training cutoff via internet access), analyzes popular accounts, even writes and refactors code to generate a poll, and finally writes a text file of concrete steps to grow a Twitter account.

Throughout, Cole surfaces recurring lessons: run in step-by-step mode so you can authorize each action and abort if the agent does something dumb or dangerous (rather than continuous mode); 'garbage in, garbage out' â€” good prompts matter, and here the AI even generates better prompts for itself; and the whole thing is experimental and imperfect (it loops, it stays too general) but is 'the start of something big' that will compound as models and tooling improve.

key_moments.note: Defines Auto-GPT: 'Auto' = autonomous; you give it a task and it generates and executes its own steps
Frames Auto-GPT as 'sparks of AGI' â€” AI that thinks, acts, and sets its own goals
Walks through setup: Python 3.8+, OpenAI API key, optional Pinecone (memory) and ElevenLabs (voice)
Continuous mode vs. step-by-step authorization; step mode lets you oversee and abort risky actions
Live demo begins: defines Twitter-GPT's role and three goals
Agent self-criticizes to think from different angles before choosing the next action
Agent browses Twitter, then falls back to Google search â€” overcoming GPT's Sept-2021 cutoff via internet access
Agent generates its own improved prompt for GPT ('garbage in, garbage out')
Final output: a text file of concrete steps to grow a Twitter account

sources.title: How to Use GPT4 To Grow on Twitter The Right Way
sources.thesis: Good prompt engineering â€” setting a role, giving context and format constraints, then having the model critique and rewrite its own output â€” turns generic, cringey ChatGPT tweet replies into human-sounding ones.
sources.summary:
In this early (April 2023) video, Cole introduces prompt engineering through a concrete use case: generating good Twitter reply drafts for his 'Tweet Genie' feature inside Cardinal/Kernel branding software. His central rule is that 'the quality of your output is determined by the quality of your input': a bare ask like 'give me YouTube video ideas' yields bland, jumbled results, while a prompt that adds context, descriptive adjectives (original, engaging), and an explicit output format ('a list of 10 ideas') gets usable output.

He walks through his GPT-4 prompt for tweet replies, which starts by telling the model who it is ('you are an expert at growing Twitter accounts through engaging and insightful tweets and replies'), then asks for an informal, engaging reply without hashtags and under 280 characters. The first reply is decent but too formal, so he applies the 'critic model' pattern: a second prompt that reassigns the model as an open, opinionated Critic and expert, feeds it the original tweet and the prior reply, asks it to critique and suggest improvements, then rewrite â€” forcing step-by-step self-reflection that produces a more human result.

A key aside: because he'll ship this through the OpenAI API rather than the ChatGPT UI, every request must carry all its own context. The UI remembers prior conversation, but an API call is stateless â€” the model only knows what you send it (unless you use a fine-tuned model, which he defers to a later video). This is why he re-pastes the tweet and reply into the critic prompt instead of relying on remembered context.

key_moments.note: Defines prompt engineering as crafting prompts so the AI understands what you want
Core rule: quality of output is determined by quality of input
Improve prompts with context, descriptive adjectives, and explicit output format
Motivating use case: Tweet Genie, avoiding bland/cringey third-person, hashtag-heavy replies
Walks through the role-setting reply prompt (expert Twitter grower, informal, no hashtags, <280 chars)
Introduces the critic model â€” having the AI criticize its own reply
API statelessness: an API request only knows what you give it, unlike the UI
Second (critiqued) reply sounds more human than the first
